questions.text: A startup’s HR team routinely skips reviewing position descriptions before shortlisting candidates, assuming they already understand the job’s requirements. Based on the discussion, what is the most likely consequence of this practice?
options: Hiring misfits who do not align with evolving role expectations
Increased diversity in candidate selection
Higher speed of recruitment with no impact on outcomes
Lower compensation costs due to faster onboarding
questions.explanation: Without a clear and current JD, recruiters lack the yardstick needed to assess if a candidate's skills match the actual needs.

questions.text: During a hiring cycle, the selection panel uses outdated position descriptions that no longer reflect new organisational priorities. Which action would best reflect a human resource manager’s mindset as suggested in the session?
options: Proceed with the hiring process and adjust the candidate’s role later.
Rely on informal knowledge of the team to assess candidates’ fit.
Update the position description to reflect current expectations before advertising or interviewing.
Focus recruitment only on past employees familiar with the old expectations.
questions.explanation: The HR manager should ensure the recruitment tools (JDs) are aligned with strategy before looking for talent.

questions.text: An entrepreneur allows each department to independently collect and evaluate job applications without a centralised tracking system. Based on the discussion, what is the most likely consequence of this approach?
options: Missed opportunities to identify suitable candidates due to scattered and untracked data
Increased applicant diversity but reduced selection time
Enhanced accountability through departmental autonomy
Improved specialisation in hiring decisions
questions.explanation: Decentralized hiring often leads to inefficiency where one department's rejected candidate might have been perfect for another.

questions.text: An entrepreneurial firm conducts multiple interviews for each candidate but notices inconsistent hiring decisions, strong candidates are rejected in one round and shortlisted in another. Based on the discussion, what is the most likely root cause of this problem?
options: Lack of standardised assessment tools such as trade or technical tests
Excessive focus on cultural fit over technical competence
Repetition of interview questions across panels
Unchecked interviewer biases and lack of structured selection process
questions.explanation: Inconsistent decisions usually stem from interviewers using subjective criteria rather than a structured assessment rubric.

questions.text: Why is selection considered a crucial aspect of building organisational culture?
options: It is the easiest part of being an entrepreneur.
It determines the founder’s philosophy.
It shapes the culture and high-performance nature of the organisation
It allows founders to avoid participating in interviews.
questions.explanation: Hiring determines who enters the organization; these individuals will either reinforce or dilute the desired culture.

questions.text: Two startups hire equally skilled employees, but one founder personally participates in every interview, while the other does not. Over time, the first startup demonstrates stronger cultural cohesion. Based on the video, what explains this difference?
options: The first startup’s founder uses structured technical tests to ensure fairness.
The founder’s personal presence ensures faster decision-making and reduces recruitment time.
Employees feel more motivated when interviewed directly by the founder, improving short-term productivity.
Founder-led hiring transmits the founding philosophy and reinforces shared values through the selection process.
questions.explanation: A founder's involvement in hiring serves as a primary mechanism for communicating and embedding the firm's core values.

questions.text: One of the aspects of high performance culture is communicating your vision clearly with everyone in your organisation. Which of the below is the intended result of that communication?
options: To increase your visibility in the organisation
To ensure all employees have an understanding of your aspiration for the organisation
To encourage free and fair communication across the organisation
All of the above
questions.explanation: Vision communication aligns individual efforts with the founder's long-term aspirations for the firm.

questions.text: An entrepreneur complains that despite hiring skilled employees, the organisation’s performance remains inconsistent. Based on the discussion, which missing element is most likely affecting performance?
options: Lack of clear communication of the organisation’s vision and individual roles within it
Inadequate investment in high-end technology
Excessive delegation of work to mid-level managers
Overemphasis on recognition and rewards
questions.explanation: Performance requires both skill and alignment; without a clear vision, even skilled employees may work in conflicting directions.

questions.text: What is one of the key distinctions made in the session regarding success in entrepreneurial organisations?
options: Entrepreneur success is always equal to enterprise success.
Entrepreneur success and enterprise success are the same in the initial stages but diverge as the organisation scales.
Enterprise success is more important than entrepreneur success at all times.
Entrepreneur success is independent of enterprise success from the beginning.
questions.explanation: While initially tied, as a company scales, the success of the 'business' must eventually become distinct from the personal success of the 'founder'.

questions.text: Which of the following dimensions are emphasized in the session for effective performance management in entrepreneurial organisations? (Select all that apply)
options: Organisational performance management
Planning and goal setting
Financial management and budgeting
Measuring employee performance
questions.explanation: Effective management focuses on aligning goals, tracking organizational health, and evaluating individual contributions.

questions.text: Which of the following is crucial for scaling an organisation effectively?
options: IT infrastructure
Physical infrastructure
Tools and working equipment
All of the above
questions.explanation: Scaling requires robust underlying infrastructure (digital, physical, and tools) to support an increasing volume of work.

questions.text: An organisation reports high profits and returning customers but struggles with employee retention and leadership depth. Based on the five performance dimensions, which area is most in need of attention?
options: Financial perspective
Customer perspective
Organisational perspective
Compliance perspective
questions.explanation: Retention and leadership pipeline are internal health indicators belonging to the 'Organisational perspective'.

questions.text: A founder’s goal sheet consists of 80% business-as-usual targets, 15% long-term goals, and 5% personal development. Based on the framework, what is the most likely consequence of this imbalance?
options: Faster operational results and better employee engagement
Strong long-term innovation but weak short-term delivery
Organisational stagnation due to overemphasis on immediate tasks
Strong long-term innovation but weak short-term delivery
questions.explanation: Over-focusing on daily operations (BAU) prevents the strategic planning and innovation needed for long-term growth.

questions.text: During goal cascading, a leadership team ensures each departmental goal reflects financial, customer, organisational, strategic, and compliance dimensions. What key leadership principle does this exemplify?
options: Cross-functional collaboration
Employee autonomy
Centralised control
Comprehensive goal alignment
questions.explanation: Goal alignment ensures that every level of the organization is working toward the same balanced set of priorities.

questions.text: A founder conducts annual appraisals but rarely provides quarterly feedback or development conversations. What key gap exists in their performance management approach?
options: Overemphasis on operational efficiency
Absence of ongoing coaching that drives behavioural and skill alignment
Excessive delegation to HR departments
Lack of financial transparency during evaluations
questions.explanation: Performance management is a continuous process; annual reviews without coaching fail to correct behavior in real-time.

questions.text: During evaluation discussions, an entrepreneur uses the session to understand an employee’s aspirations and explore job rotation options for future growth. This reflects which core principle of effective performance management?
options: Linking rewards strictly to measurable KPIs
Delegating evaluation entirely to HR managers
Encouraging competition among team members
Using evaluations as forward-looking development and succession planning tools
questions.explanation: Modern evaluations are not just about past scores but about planning the employee's future growth within the firm.

questions.text: An entrepreneur focuses solely on profits and organisational expansion but neglects personal growth and self-care. According to the “living the legacy” framework, which key dimension is being ignored?
options: Managerial effectiveness
Entrepreneurial effectiveness
Enterprise effectiveness
Personal effectiveness
questions.explanation: Personal effectiveness involves the founder's own well-being, learning, and self-management.

questions.text: A founder designs systems that allow the business to run smoothly without daily involvement and mentors a leadership team to take over future decisions. This demonstrates:
options: Managerial efficiency but weak entrepreneurial vision
Strong personal effectiveness but limited managerial focus
Over-delegation leading to loss of control
Entrepreneurial effectiveness contributing to enterprise effectiveness
questions.explanation: Entrepreneurial effectiveness includes building an institution that survives and thrives independently of the founder.

questions.text: What is implied as a critical factor for maintaining effectiveness as an entrepreneur scales their business?
options: Developing advanced technical skills
Continuously increasing market share
Maintaining a high level of self-awareness and reflecting on how they are perceived by others
Expanding the product line to meet diverse customer needs
questions.explanation: Self-awareness is vital to ensure the founder's changing role doesn't become a bottleneck for the organization.

questions.text: Which of the following statements best captures the potential downside of an entrepreneur's strengths?
options: Strengths often remain consistent and beneficial regardless of business growth.
Strengths, when overstretched, can transform into weaknesses that may hinder business progress.
Entrepreneurs' strengths typically have no impact on their weaknesses.
Strengths are always the primary factor in an entrepreneur's long-term success.
questions.explanation: A strength like 'attention to detail' can become a 'micromanagement' weakness as the organization grows.

questions.text: What underlying principle is emphasised by the need for managers to continuously communicate their vision and purpose to their employees?
options: Ensuring employees follow orders without question
Creating a shared understanding and commitment to the organisation's goals
Reducing the need for formal training programs
Increasing the speed of task completion
questions.explanation: Continuous communication builds 'mental models' that help employees make the right decisions autonomously.

questions.text: Why is it crucial for managers to lead by example in an organisation?
options: To reduce the need for employee oversight
To ensure they complete their own tasks on time
To serve as a role model and build credibility among employees
To decrease the likelihood of employee turnover
questions.explanation: Leaders lose credibility if their actions contradict the culture they are trying to build.

questions.text: Which aspect of personal effectiveness is emphasised as a casualty of an entrepreneur's busy schedule when scaling their business?
options: Delegation skills
Financial management
Active listening
Marketing strategies
questions.explanation: Under time pressure, founders often stop listening and start 'telling', which alienates staff and blocks important information.

questions.text: Which of the following behaviours is highlighted as detrimental to an entrepreneur's ability to scale their business effectively?
options: Over-delegating tasks to team members
Frequently changing business strategies
Displaying intolerance and anger towards employees who do not understand instructions immediately
Setting overly ambitious financial goals
questions.explanation: Intolerance and emotional outbursts create a culture of fear, which suppresses employee initiative and growth.

questions.text: How can an entrepreneur's legacy influence the broader community?
options: By creating personal wealth for the founder.
By ensuring the company remains a small, closely-held business.
By growing the business and creating wealth for all stakeholders involved.
By focusing solely on the company's profitability.
questions.explanation: A positive entrepreneurial legacy involves shared prosperity for employees, vendors, and the community.

questions.text: What is implied about the importance of reflecting on the legacy one leaves behind as an entrepreneur?
options: It is a secondary concern to daily operational tasks.
It ensures that only personal wealth is accumulated.
It primarily focuses on maintaining the initial business philosophy without change.
It involves recognising and shaping the impact on the community, stakeholders, and future of the business.
questions.explanation: Legacy thinking prompts founders to act ethically and build sustainable systems that outlast their direct tenure.

questions.text: Why is it important for entrepreneurs to periodically re-examine their fundamental values and beliefs?
options: To ensure they remain static and unchanging.
To adapt and change based on the external environment.
To negotiate and compromise on core values.
To avoid becoming extinct like dinosaurs.
questions.explanation: Adaptability in values ensures the founder can lead the organization through different stages of industry and social evolution.

questions.text: Which statement best summarises the approach recommended for leaving a legacy as an entrepreneur?
options: Focusing solely on building successors.
Making decisions and taking actions with ethics in mind.
Maintaining a strict adherence to initial business philosophies.
Creating opportunities without regard for the external environment.
questions.explanation: A legacy is built on a foundation of integrity and decisions that prioritize long-term social and business value.

questions.text: According to the lecture, why can’t line managers independently hire or promote employees without involving the HR department?
options: Because HR has the final authority to make all hiring decisions
Because HRM ensures fairness, consistency, and regulatory compliance across the organisation
Because HRM policies focus solely on recruitment efficiency
Because managers lack the skills to assess candidates effectively
questions.explanation: HR functions as a coordinating body that ensures all personnel decisions meet legal standards and organizational standards of fairness.

questions.text: The analogy between HRM policies and traffic lights emphasises which key principle of organisational management?
options: HRM restricts managerial decision-making freedom
HRM enables structured behavior through coordination and control mechanisms
HRM prioritises employee satisfaction over organisational needs
HRM reduces accountability among managers
questions.explanation: Policies act as 'traffic lights' that provide the rules of the road, enabling many managers to work independently without causing chaos.

questions.text: According to Story’s (1995) definition discussed in the lecture, HRM achieves competitive advantage primarily through:
options: Strategic deployment of a highly committed and capable workforce
Increasing automation and reducing workforce dependency
Enforcing rigid organisational policies across departments
Offering high compensation to attract top performers
questions.explanation: HRM is strategic when it aligns employee capabilities and commitment with the firm's goal for market dominance.

questions.text: The phrase “an integrated array of cultural, structural, and personal techniques” in Story’s HRM definition signifies which of the following?
options: HRM should function independently of organizational culture and structure
HRM focuses mainly on personnel techniques like hiring and compensation
HRM policies are effective only in bureaucratic organisations
HRM combines people practices, culture, and structure into a unified strategic system
questions.explanation: Effective HRM is not just about 'hiring'; it is about how culture and reporting lines work together to manage people.

questions.text: According to the session, treating people purely as a cost can negatively affect which of the following aspects of organisational growth?
options: Employee commitment and innovation capacity
Cost efficiency and budget control
Leadership hierarchy and reporting structure
Financial auditing and compliance accuracy
questions.explanation: When seen only as costs, employees become disengaged, reducing their willingness to contribute ideas or extra effort.

questions.text: The concept of human capital, as explained in the lecture, emphasises which of the following forms of value creation within organisations?
options: Reducing salary expenditure through automation
Enhancing organisational value through intellectual, human, and social capital
Standardising HR processes for efficiency and uniformity
Increasing workforce turnover to maintain competitive pressure
questions.explanation: Human capital recognizes that the collective knowledge and relationships of employees are valuable economic assets.

questions.text: According to the session, HR strategies contribute to business outcomes primarily through which sequence of relationships?
options: HR Strategy → Financial Outcomes → HR Outcomes → Employee Behavior
HR Strategy → HR Department Efficiency → Business Outcomes → Employee Satisfaction
HR Strategy → Recruitment Volume → Market Share → Profitability
HR Strategy → Employee Attributes → HR Outcomes → Business Outcomes
questions.explanation: Strategies improve employee skills (attributes), leading to better engagement (outcomes), which finally drives business results.

questions.text: An organisation observes declining customer satisfaction despite competitive compensation and regular training programs. Based on the HR strategy–outcome–business outcome model, which area should be examined first?
options: HR outcomes such as employee engagement and punctuality
Financial reporting and profit allocation
External recruitment advertising and branding
Market-level product differentiation strategy
questions.explanation: Intermediate HR outcomes like engagement are the 'missing link' between policies and business results.

questions.text: In the 5P model of HRM, which of the following best illustrates the connection between philosophy and policies?
options: Launching a high-potential program to attract external candidates
Defining performance management and training policies that support internal talent growth
Creating communication templates for HR announcements
Setting up a digital portal for employee nominations
questions.explanation: A philosophy of 'valuing internal growth' must be concretely supported by policies that enable training and internal promotion.

questions.text: An organisation has a philosophy of promoting from within but often hires externally for leadership roles. According to the 5P model, this issue reflects misalignment between which dimensions?
options: Philosophy and policies
Programs and processes
Practices and communication
Processes and compensation
questions.explanation: Misalignment occurs when the stated beliefs (philosophy) are contradicted by the actual rules and guidelines (policies).

questions.text: During an interview, a manager repeatedly interrupts the candidate and answers most questions himself to demonstrate expertise. According to the 3 C’s framework, which principle is being violated, and what is the likely consequence?
options: Context – The physical setup is uncomfortable, leading to poor attendance.
Content – The interviewer lacked the right questions, leading to an unstructured process.
Conduct – The interviewer failed to create dignity and space, damaging candidate perception.
Communication – The interviewer spoke excessively, reducing interview efficiency.
questions.explanation: Conduct refers to the behavior of the panelists; disrespectful behavior damages the employer's reputation.

questions.text: A candidate shares negative feedback on social media, saying the interviewers seemed unprepared and unaware of the job requirements. Which aspect of the 3 C’s was most likely violated, and what cultural signal does this send to potential applicants?
options: Context – The organization lacks a welcoming environment; signals poor facilities.
Conduct – The organisation discourages questioning; signals arrogance.
Coordination – The organisation lacks collaboration among panelists; signals inefficiency.
Content – The organisation is unclear about role expectations; signals weak professionalism.
questions.explanation: Content refers to the substance of the interview; being unprepared indicates a lack of professionalism in the firm's operations.

questions.text: According to the session, performance management in organisations primarily functions as:
options: A reward distribution mechanism for high performers
A strategy implementation tracker aligning organisational and individual goals
A system for documenting employee behaviour for HR audits
A communication platform between HR and employees
questions.explanation: Performance management ensures that every employee's effort is directed toward achieving the company's strategic plan.

questions.text: A company finds that while strategic goals are ambitious, employee objectives remain vague and unconnected to larger plans. Which weakness in the performance management process does this reflect?
options: Overemphasis on financial metrics
Excessive focus on quarterly reviews
Insufficient recognition and rewards system
Lack of cascading alignment between organisational and individual goals
questions.explanation: Cascading involves breaking down high-level corporate goals into specific, relevant targets for every individual.

questions.text: A team member consistently delivers strong results in one area but hasn’t explored broader roles. The manager identifies this employee as high performer, moderate potential. According to the session, what is the most effective managerial intervention?
options: Rotate them into adjacent roles to expand their breadth and uncover hidden potential.
Provide short-term incentives to sustain current performance levels.
Keep them focused on their specialisation to ensure consistent delivery.
Assign them a leadership role immediately to accelerate development.
questions.explanation: Job rotation helps test if a 'steady performer' has the capacity to take on more complex or varied responsibilities.

questions.text: While mapping team members on the performance- potential matrix, a manager relies only on personal judgment without seeking feedback from peers or other supervisors. What risk does this pose to effective career development?
options: It reduces training costs by focusing on observable performance only.
It ensures faster decision-making but improves fairness.
It introduces bias and undermines data-driven talent decisions.
It aligns with organisational autonomy in people decisions.
questions.explanation: Subjective mapping without multiple viewpoints (calibration) leads to favoritism and unfair talent decisions.

questions.text: A manager discovers that one of her team members has repeatedly violated attendance norms. She decides to “let it go” to avoid confrontation, assuming the issue will self-correct. Based on the disciplinary framework discussed, what managerial mistake is being made?
options: Failure to apply the Hot Stove Rule, by delaying immediate action and losing credibility.
Violation of natural justice, by not giving the employee a chance to explain.
Over-documentation, creating unnecessary administrative complexity.
Application of inconsistent punishment, undermining fairness.
questions.explanation: The Hot Stove Rule states discipline should be immediate; delaying action encourages others to violate rules too.

questions.text: During a disciplinary inquiry, a manager who was directly involved in the incident takes charge of judging the case. Which principle of natural justice is being violated, and what cultural risk does this pose?
options: Presumption of innocence – leading to employee distrust and bias in evaluation.
Impartiality (No one should be a judge in their own case) – leading to perceived unfairness and cultural erosion.
Right to appeal – leading to HR procedural delays.
Progressive penalty principle – leading to inconsistent punishment.
questions.explanation: Natural justice requires an unbiased arbiter to ensure that investigations and punishments are seen as fair.

questions.text: A manager ensures that each team member’s goals are directly derived from departmental and organisational objectives. According to the concept discussed, what core principle of performance management is being practiced?
options: Feedback and appraisal consistency
Motivational reinforcement via rewards
Competency-based evaluation
Strategic alignment through goal cascading
questions.explanation: Strategic alignment is achieved when every individual's targets clearly support the higher-level mission.

questions.text: A manager excels at setting clear goals but avoids giving feedback due to discomfort with critical conversations. Based on the three key aspects of managing performance, which area of vulnerability does this reveal, and what long-term impact might it have?
options: Weakness in coaching; it limits opportunities for employee learning and growth.
Weakness in review and feedback; it hampers performance culture and continuous improvement.
Weakness in goal setting; it misaligns individual and team goals.
Weakness in motivation; it leads to high attrition and disengagement.
questions.explanation: Goal setting without feedback is incomplete; employees need to know how they are progressing to adjust their efforts.

questions.text: A manager assigns her team goals that include both increasing quarterly sales by 15% and fostering stronger interdepartmental collaboration. According to the principles of goal setting discussed, what key managerial competency is being demonstrated?
options: Prioritisation of short-term financial outcomes
Balancing quantitative and qualitative objectives for holistic performance
Delegating goal ownership to HR for standardisation
Avoiding intangible goals to maintain measurable accountability
questions.explanation: Effective goals cover both the 'what' (numeric targets) and the 'how' (behavioral/cultural expectations).

questions.text: In a team where all goals are strictly numeric—focused on revenue, production, and deadlines, employees show declining morale and collaboration. Based on the session’s insights, what is the most likely cause?
options: Goals are not SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
The team lacks adequate resources to achieve high targets.
The absence of qualitative goals has undermined behavioural and cultural alignment.
Annual goals are not linked to departmental objectives.
questions.explanation: Over-focusing on numbers often leads to 'toxic' productivity where teamwork and ethics are sacrificed to meet targets.

questions.text: Why is it important for managers to consider interdependencies when setting goals for employees?
options: It simplifies the goal-setting process
It ensures all employees have the same type of goals.
It helps in understanding the challenges faced by employees with higher interdependencies.
It allows managers to set more quantitative goals
questions.explanation: Some employees rely heavily on others for their results; ignoring this can lead to unfair performance ratings.

questions.text: A manager fails to document changes made to employee goals mid-year, later realising some critical assignments were reprioritised. According to the principles discussed, what is the most significant risk this oversight creates?
options: Reduced employee motivation due to lack of clarity
Inability to link performance outcomes to compensation
Overemphasis on qualitative goals over quantitative ones
Perceived unfairness and loss of credibility in evaluation
questions.explanation: Evaluating someone against outdated goals is unfair and damages the trust between the manager and the subordinate.

questions.text: Which of the following are key components of an effective coaching session? (Select all that apply)
options: Stock taking and reviewing progress
Focusing only on positive feedback.
Reclassifying and reprioritising goals.
Discussing enabling and disabling performance factors.
questions.explanation: Coaching involves reviewing data, identifying barriers, and adjusting plans for the future.

questions.text: What is John Whitmore's definition of coaching?
options: Teaching employees new skills.
Unlocking a person's potential to maximise their own performance.
Setting goals for employees to achieve
Monitoring employees' performance regularly.
questions.explanation: Whitmore’s classic definition emphasizes enabling the individual to find their own solutions and peak performance.

questions.text: A manager schedules a performance review but takes calls and checks messages during the meeting. Based on the principles of review and feedback discussed, which critical leadership responsibility is being compromised?
options: Providing developmental feedback
Ensuring accurate documentation of performance
Establishing psychological safety and respect
Setting performance goals for the next cycle
questions.explanation: Giving undivided attention during a review signal respect and creates the safety needed for honest dialogue.

questions.text: During an annual appraisal, an employee feels the manager’s assessment is biased and inconsistent with prior discussions. According to the transcript, what is most likely missing from the manager’s approach?
options: Regular quarterly coaching and documentation
Objective goal alignment with team targets
Clear communication about compensation policy
Formal approval from the HR department
questions.explanation: Continuous coaching and recording of achievements prevent the 'surprise' factor during annual appraisals.

questions.text: Which of the following is a key aspect of managing performance?
options: Focusing solely on achieving organisational goals
Ignoring employees' career aspirations
Identifying growth and development opportunities for team members
Conducting annual reviews without regular feedback sessions
questions.explanation: Performance management is a dual process of achieving goals and growing human potential.

questions.text: What should managers do to support employees' career growth during the goal-setting process?
options: Avoid setting new and challenging goals
Help employees to create development plans and acquire new skills
Limit employees' responsibilities to their current roles
Focus only on meeting current targets
questions.explanation: Strategic goal setting includes 'stretch' targets that require employees to learn and expand their professional horizon.

questions.text: Which of the following best captures the central paradox of technology highlighted in the module?
options: Technology increases efficiency but permanently eliminates most human jobs
Technology enhances effectiveness while simultaneously weakening the very human capabilities required to work effectively with it
Technology mainly impacts blue-collar work, leaving managerial work unchanged
Technology replaces tacit knowledge with explicit, codified knowledge
questions.explanation: The module emphasizes that while technology aids productivity, it can erode essential human skills like deep focus and spatial navigation that are needed to manage technology.

questions.text: A manager relies heavily on AI tools, GPS navigation, dashboards, and short-form content for decision-making and information consumption. According to the ideas discussed in the module, which risk is most likely to emerge over time?
options: Reduced technical proficiency and digital fluency
Overdependence on social intelligence and emotional skills
Decline in deep focus, pattern recognition, and independent judgment
Increased resistance to technological adoption
questions.explanation: Constant use of restrictive digital tools can rewire the brain to process information as fleeting memories, reducing the capacity for deep work and pattern recognition.

questions.text: The opening question, “How global are we?”, primarily challenges which common misconception about globalisation?
options: Being global requires international travel or physical relocation
Globalisation only affects large multinational corporations, not individuals
Globalisation is driven mainly by political agreements between countries
Globalisation eliminates cultural differences across regions
questions.explanation: The session argues that being global is defined by our consumption and participation in global value chains, regardless of whether we have traveled abroad.

questions.text: A student argues that since they plan to work for an Indian company in India, understanding cross-cultural dimensions is unnecessary. Based on the module, which response best counters this argument?
options: Most Indian firms now follow Western management practices, making culture irrelevant
Cultural differences matter only when dealing with foreign clients or expatriates
Indian organisations are increasingly part of global value chains and interact with diverse national and regional cultures
Cultural competence is useful mainly for leadership roles, not entry-level employees
questions.explanation: Domestic firms are often suppliers or partners in global networks, requiring an understanding of both international and diverse internal sub-cultures.

questions.text: Why does the module emphasise examples such as the East India Company and early multinational brands operating in India before independence?
options: To highlight the economic dominance of foreign firms over Indian markets
To show that globalisation is a long-standing phenomenon rather than a recent trend
To argue that modern globalisation is identical to colonial trade systems
To suggest that cultural differences were insignificant in early global business
questions.explanation: The speaker points out that many global brands have operated in India for over a century, illustrating that global interaction is a deeply rooted concept.

questions.text: A student believes that exposure to global pop culture (music, fashion, films) has reduced the importance of national culture in business contexts. Based on the module, which response best addresses this view?
options: Global pop culture standardises consumer behaviour, making culture largely irrelevant
National culture matters only in traditional industries, not modern global brands
Popular culture replaces national culture in shaping workplace behaviour
Cultural differences persist and continue to influence values, behaviours, and responses to products and services
questions.explanation: Despite the spread of pop culture, deep-seated national norms and values continue to dictate how people perceive work, authority, and communication.

questions.text: The "birth lottery" thought experiment is primarily used in the module to illustrate which foundational idea underlying cross-cultural understanding?
options: Individual success is determined mainly by effort and talent
Cultural differences are temporary and disappear with globisation
Life outcomes are shaped by contextual factors beyond individual control
Economic systems are more influential than cultural systems
questions.explanation: The Warren Buffett analogy demonstrates that the diverse circumstances we are born into determine the cultural programming we receive.

questions.text: Which statement best reflects Hofstede's idea of culture as a "shared programming of the mind", as discussed in the lecture?
options: Culture is a set of individual personality traits that carry randomly across people
Culture is fixed at birth and cannot evolve over time
Culture consists mainly of visible symbols such as food, dress, and language
Culture represents deeply embedded patterns of thinking and behaviour learned through socialisation
questions.explanation: Hofstede defines culture as a collective mental program passed through societal norms that structures how a group sees the world.

questions.text: A multinational manager notices that employees in one country rarely challenge senior leaders, expect clear hierarchical direction, and accept unequal distribution of authority as normal. Based on Hofstede’s framework, which cultural dimension best explains this behaviour?
options: High uncertainty avoidance
High collectivism
High power distance
High masculinity
questions.explanation: Power distance refers to the degree to which a society accepts and expects unequal distribution of power and hierarchical structures.

questions.text: Which of the following scenarios best illustrates a high collectivism orientation as described in the lecture?
options: Career decisions are influenced by family expectations and community considerations
Employees prioritise personal achievement and individual performance bonuses
Organisations reward assertiveness, competition, and material success
Individuals are comfortable making decisions under ambiguous conditions
questions.explanation: Collectivism is characterized by strong interpersonal ties where individuals feel responsible for the group and community expectations influence decisions.

questions.text: Which of the following best captures the core trade-off of India’s low uncertainty avoidance as discussed in the lecture?
options: High comfort with ambiguity encourages innovation but reduces social cohesion
High tolerance for uncertainty enables adaptability but may weaken long-term planning
Low fear of uncertainty leads to rigid rule-following and resistance to change
Comfort with uncertainty results in excessive risk-taking and poor teamwork
questions.explanation: The lecture notes that while India's high tolerance for ambiguity makes change easy, it can lead to under-investment in detailed long-term planning.

questions.text: A young Indian professional struggles to challenge a senior leader’s flawed decision, despite recognising the issue early. Which combination of Hofstede’s dimensions most directly explains this behaviour?
options: High masculinity and high uncertainty avoidance
Low power distance and high collectivism
High uncertainty avoidance and low masculinity
High power distance and balanced individualism–collectivism
questions.explanation: India's high power distance score indicates a deference to age and experience, which can make challenging superiors difficult despite recognized errors.

questions.text: A professional from a high power-distance culture joins a team where hierarchy is minimal and junior members openly challenge leaders. Which difficulty is most likely to arise initially, based on the session?
options: Discomfort in expressing dissent and influencing authority
Reduced motivation due to weak incentives
Difficulty working in teams due to individualism
Lack of technical competence
questions.explanation: Individuals from hierarchical cultures often struggle to put their points across or influence those they perceive as more senior.

questions.text: Why do mergers and acquisitions often fail despite strong financial and strategic alignment, according to the discussion on national culture?
options: Differences in compensation laws alone create integration challenges
Deep-seated cultural differences affect perceptions, behaviours, and management practices
Individual personality clashes outweigh organisational concerns
Employees resist change due to uncertainty avoidance in all cultures
questions.explanation: The headquarters' national culture significantly influences practices; clashing cultural norms often become a barrier to successful integration.

questions.text: Which behaviour best reflects the mindset of an effective global professional, as illustrated by examples in the session?
options: Avoiding unfamiliar situations to minimise the risk of cultural mistakes
Relying on prior cultural knowledge to interpret all situations confidently
Maintaining emotional distance to prevent personal discomfort in cross-cultural interactions
Acknowleding lack of understanding, apologising when needed, and learning from mistakes
questions.explanation: Global professionalism involves living with ambiguity, showing respect, and having the humility to learn from context-specific social errors.

questions.text: In the story involving German family and the suitcase, why does the speaker stress the need to be non-judgemental rather than intellectually informed?
options: Historical knowledge can create emotional detachment in cross-cultural settings
Global professionals should avoid discussing sensitive topics altogether
Cultural differences eliminate the need for rational understanding
Emotional contexts require suspending analysis and responding with empathy
questions.explanation: Being non-judgmental involves recognizing emotional moments as separate from intellectual ones and responding with human empathy rather than analysis.

questions.text: Which statement best reflects the session’s core position on ethics versus legality in organisational contexts?
options: Legal compliance is necessary but insufficient for ethical business conduct
Ethical behaviour is ensured as long as organisations comply with laws and regulations
Ethics applies only in situations where laws are unclear or absent
Ethics primarily exists to protect organisations from legal penalties
questions.explanation: The speaker explicitly states that ethics is beyond compliance and way beyond the legal minimum threshold prescribed by law.

questions.text: A marketing professional is asked to promote a legally approved product that the organisation internally knows has harmful side effects. According to the ethical framework discussed, which concern should take priority in deciding how to act?
options: Whether the product complies with all regulatory standards
Whether refusing the task may harm personal career prospects
Whether promoting the product violates the principle of “do no harm”
Whether competitors are engaging in similar practices
questions.explanation: Ethical standards require making sure we 'do no harm' to individuals before considering the positive outcomes or legal status.

questions.text: A young entrepreneur is asked by an investor to hide certain risks while raising funds. Using the ethical approach discussed in the lecture, which question is most likely to lead to the right course of action?
options: Will this help the business survive in the short term?
Is this practice common in the startup ecosystem?
What is the worst that could happen if this information becomes public?
Can this decision be justified legally if questioned?
questions.explanation: Asking 'what is the worst that can happen' if an action is discovered helps clarify the impact on reputation and dictates the right choice.

questions.text: A global manager operates in a country where gift-giving is culturally accepted as a sign of respect, but company policy strictly limits gifts to prevent bribery. Based on the session, what is the most ethically appropriate response?
options: Fully adapt to local norms, as ethical behaviour is culture-specific
Reject all gifts to avoid any ethical ambiguity
Accept gifts privately to preserve relationships without violating rules publicly
Follow organisational standards while showing cultural sensitivity in non-material ways
questions.explanation: The session suggests standardizing acceptable gift-giving behavior to manage ethical risks across different cultural contexts.

questions.text: According to the session, if a manager does not have someone ready to take over their role, what does this indicate about their career progression?
options: The manager is indispensable and therefore highly valued by the organisation.
The manager’s career may have plateaued due to over-dependence on their own indispensability.
The manager is demonstrating strong personal accountability and reliability.
The manager is effectively developing long-term leadership capacity.
questions.explanation: The speaker argues that being too indispensable prevents growth; a manager's first agenda should be creating a successor to free themselves for new roles.

questions.text: The speaker suggests answering three reflection questions annually, not during appraisal cycles to enhance personal effectiveness. What is the key rationale for this recommendation?
options: Appraisal cycles are performance-focused, while self-reflection should be personally motivated and ongoing.
Reflecting during appraisals can bias performance ratings.
Managers have more free time outside appraisal periods to reflect deeply.
Organisational processes discourage personal development discussions during appraisals.
questions.explanation: Reflection should be a personal journey for growth, whereas appraisals are formal organizational requirements often tied to rewards.

questions.text: According to the session, which of the following most accurately represents a key indicator of a manager’s effectiveness?
options: The strength of the manager’s highest-performing team members
The number of projects the team completes on time
The performance of the weakest link or least effective member in the team
The amount of time spent mentoring top performers
questions.explanation: A manager is responsible for the entire team; the weakest link determines the overall strength of the team's 'chain.'

questions.text: The speaker observes that most managers spend over 80% of their time on the top 25% of performers and low performers combined. What critical managerial insight does this reveal?
options: High and low performers require equal amounts of supervision to maintain balance.
Average performers often receive insufficient attention, risking a decline in performance or engagement.
Managers should prioritise only high performers for efficiency.
Underperformers benefit the most from time-intensive interventions.
questions.explanation: The 'large middle' (70% of the org) often deliver well but risk slipping into low performance if they are ignored by management.

questions.text: According to the module, why does personal development require both self-awareness and discipline?
options: Because self-awareness identifies what needs improvement, while discipline ensures consistent action toward it.
Because organisations expect both as part of annual performance reviews.
Because they are complementary traits that influence communication with peers.
Because discipline enhances technical expertise while self-awareness improves knowledge retention.
questions.explanation: Knowing what to change is just the start; the speaker notes that discipline is the hardest part required to translate intent into action.

questions.text: The speaker notes that many traits of effective managers are “soft skills,” calling it “an irony.” What does this irony suggest about managerial effectiveness?
options: That technical expertise and domain knowledge are no longer necessary in management.
That organisations prioritise measurable outcomes over interpersonal relationships.
That soft skills are unrelated to leadership success but useful in teamwork.
That behavioral skills are undervalued because they seem easy to learn but are the hardest to master.
questions.explanation: Behavioral skills like coaching and listening are termed 'soft' yet require significant self-awareness and are more difficult to acquire than technical ones.

questions.text: According to the session, what is the primary purpose of creating an action plan?
options: To formally record performance feedback received from others.
To communicate one’s career goals to the HR department.
To convert self-reflection into specific, measurable steps for personal and professional growth.
To assess others’ strengths and weaknesses within the team.
questions.explanation: Action plans capture goal-setting and the specific support needed to change behaviors identified through reflection.

questions.text: According to the discussion on developing an action plan, which of the following best captures its intended managerial purpose in the context of reflection and long-term career success?
options: To translate self-awareness into structured future goals by identifying behaviours to change, required support, and actionable steps.
To document completed tasks and measure past performance metrics.
To maintain a record of professional achievements for performance appraisals.
To set competitive benchmarks against peers for career advancement.
questions.explanation: Action plans allow individuals to think about their future deliberately by setting behavior-change goals and seeking necessary help.

questions.text: Why does the speaker descirbe time analysis as crucial for self-management in an asynchronous learning environment?
options: Because it allows learners to identify unproductive patterns and consciously reallocate time to priority tasks.
Because it helps institution track student's progress through digital analytics.
Because it provides quantitative data for grading performance.
Beacsue it elminated the need for faculty feedback.
questions.explanation: Work-hour analysis helps identify 'unconscious leaks' (like excessive social media use) so time can be protected for learning.

questions.text: According to the session, what differentiate temporary behavioural change from sustainble self-improvement?
options: Temporary change happens due to external pressure, while sustainble improvement comes from personal mindfulness and intrinsic motivation.
Temporary change results from continuous learning, while sustainable improvement results from strict time schedules.
Temporary change is performance-based, while sustainble change depends solely on feedback from others.
Temporary change is guided by mentors, while sustainble change comes only from independent work.
questions.explanation: The speaker notes that while others provide feedback, only personal mindfulness can drive the lasting internal change needed for growth.

questions.text: According to the session, why is it important to review which tasks on the checklist remain unfinished?
options: Because it helps determine which colleagues failed to assist in completing tasks.
Because it ensures that no deadlines are ever missed in the future
Because it helps in transferring leftover tasks automatically to the next week's list
Because unfinished tasks reveal patterns about one's priorities, motivation, and time management behaviours
questions.explanation: Reflecting on why certain items were not ticked off reveals if we were forced to ignore them or if they simply weren't important to us.

questions.text: The professor suggests that a weekly checklist is more effective than a daily one because it:
options: Reduces the total number of tasks required to be completed.
Focuses mainly on long-term professional goals rather than short-term activities
Allows better anticipation of energy levels, priorities, and upcoming events across multiple days.
Elminated the need for reflection or revision of priorities.
questions.explanation: Weekly planning allows one to account for distractions and energy fatigue (like post-sports events) that a daily list might miss.

questions.text: According to the module, why should learners focus most on the “Important but Not Urgent” quadrant in the Eisenhower Matrix?
options: Because these activities contribute to long-term growth, learning, and development but are often neglected due to lack of urgency.
Because these tasks usually come with tight deadlines that demand immediate action.
Because these activities can easily be delegated to others.
Because they are typically repetitive and routine, requiring minimal thought.
questions.explanation: This quadrant covers personal development and tools; because they aren't due 'now,' people often postpone them indefinitely.

questions.text: What is the speaker’s main argument about time wasters in the “Not Urgent and Not Important” quadrant?
options: They should be managed through delegation to maintain social balance.
They are necessary for stress relief and should be prioritised daily.
They should be eliminated completely, regardless of personal preferences.
They should be reduced mindfully since they consume valuable time without contributing to effectiveness or growth.
questions.explanation: Identifying and reducing time wasters like mindless social media surfing is a fundamental step in the learning journey.

questions.text: According to the module, what is the primary reason the Pomodoro Technique improves focus for individuals who get distracted easily?
options: It allows multitasking by switching between multiple short tasks in a limited time frame.
It creates short, structured bursts of focused work with fixed breaks that reinforce discipline and prevent mental fatigue.
It replaces the need for goal-setting by breaking large tasks into smaller sub-tasks automatically.
It helps increase the total number of hours worked per day through extended focus sessions.
questions.explanation: The 5-minute break acts as motivation to stay focused during the 25-minute work interval, helping individuals avoid distractions.

questions.text: How does the Premack Principle enhance time management and motivation according to the transcript?
options: It prioritises multitasking across enjoyable activities to keep energy levels high.
It recommends scheduling only preferred tasks during high-energy periods.
It encourages completing less preferred or more difficult tasks first, using enjoyable tasks as a natural reward to maintain motivation.
It allows procrastination by saving difficult tasks for when motivation is higher.
questions.explanation: Completing the harder task first provides a 'reward' effect, helping to overcome procrastination on activities one likes less.

questions.text: How does reflection contribute to behavioural change?
options: It replaces the need for external feedback by focusing solely on personal interpretation.
It involves observing others and copying their behaviours to improve performance.
It allows individuals to analyse their intentions, understand consequences, and connect feedback from different contexts to refine their actions.
It helps individuals avoid uncomfortable feedback by rationalising their decisions.
questions.explanation: Reflection involves looking at why things happened as they did and linking intentions to actual outcomes to pick up behavioral cues.

questions.text: What key insights does the professor share about emulation as a developmental method?
options: While copying other's behaviours can be useful, true growth occurs when individuals adapt and personalise these learnings to create their own managerial style.
Emulation is effective only when one perfectly replicates another person's behaviour or method.
Emulation should be avoided as it prevents originality in developing personal style.
It replaces the need for reflection and observation since it produces immediate results
questions.explanation: The speaker explains that even when 'copying' a role model, individuals should hybridise those observations with their own experiences.

questions.text: According to the session, what is the primary benefit of maintaining a consistent journaling practice over time?
options: It allows people to document daily events for memory retention and academic tracking.
It enables individuals to identify recurring behavioural patterns and understand both strengths and weaknesses through self-observation.
It replaces the need for external feedback from others.
It ensures emotional expression without the need for analysis or structure.
questions.explanation: Consistency (around 90 days) allows a person to see their reality reflected back and recognize the patterns that contribute to weaknesses.

questions.text: Why does the professor describe journaling as “seeing yourself through the eyes of the other”?
options: Because journaling requires others to review and comment on your entries.
Because it simulates feedback from peers and colleagues directly.
Because it relies on comparing one’s actions to societal norms.
Because writing about experiences helps one detach emotionally and gain an external, objective perspective on personal behaviour.
questions.explanation: Journaling allows for post-facto introspection, helping the writer look back dispassionately and from multiple perspectives.

questions.text: Why does the professor emphasise that knowing what to change is not sufficient for making lasting behavioural change?
options: Because behavioural change depends only on individual willpower and not external factors.
Because identifying areas of improvement can discourage individuals from acting.
Because awareness must be supported by consistent action, accountability, and feedback from a network of mentors and peers.
Because change only occurs through formal training and structured workshops.
questions.explanation: Intentions often fade; a strong support network of people to count on is required to translate intentions into changed behavior.

questions.text: According to the session, why are friends considered insufficient as the sole component of a professional support network?
options: Friends cannot form emotional connections necessary for personal well-being.
Friends focus mainly on career mentoring rather than personal support.
Friends typically offer too much critical feedback, which can lower self-esteem during career plateaus.
Friends often provide emotional sympathy rather than objective, actionable feedback, and may lack context about your professional challenges.
questions.explanation: Friends are often too sympathetic and take your side rather than providing the hard, actionable feedback needed for professional growth.

questions.text: According to the session, what distinguishes personal resilience from other leadership attributes?
options: It involves integrating emotional regulation, purpose, values, and energy management to sustain performance under pressure.
It focuses primarily on building technical competence and decision-making efficiency.
It is a trait that can only be developed through external leadership training programs.
It replaces the need for reflection or journaling in self-development.
questions.explanation: Resilience is described as a critical attribute involving perspectives, emotion regulation, purpose, networks, and physical energy.

questions.text: The professor connects resilience with reflection by suggesting that-
options: Reflection should be replaced by mindfulness practices that suppress emotional responses.
Journaling can be used to record, reframe, and examine emotional and ethical experiences that strengthen personal resilience.
Only external mentors can help individuals become resilient through structured evaluation.
Reflection is unrelated to resilience since resilience is an innate personality trait.
questions.explanation: Reflective questions and journaling allow a manager to reframe negative emotions and align actions with core values to build resilience.

questions.text: A person is watching an educational video while the AC hums, traffic noise seeps in, and family members talk nearby. Yet the person remains focused on the content. Which sequences best captures the internal process described?
options: Detect all stimuli → respond equally to all → fatigue
Detect stimuli → randomly select one → ignore all others
Detect multiple stimuli → selectivley attend to some → organise and interpret them → give meaning while filtering out others
Ignore all external stimuli → rely only on memory
questions.explanation: This sequence describes the stages of perception where an individual filters out distractions to focus on and interpret specific relevant information.

questions.text: A manager concludes "Rohan is careless and disrespectful", without first exploring whether norms and expectations had been clearly communicated or whether roles had recently changed. From a perceptual standpoint, what is the deepest problem with this conclusion?
options: It ignores that attitudes, past experience, and expectations shape what is noticed and how behaviour is interpreted
It assumes perception is purely sensory, not interpretive
It treats communication as more critical than perception
It assumes that norms never matter
questions.explanation: The manager's conclusion is biased as it fails to account for external factors and personal framing that influence how behavior is perceived and judged.

questions.text: A colleague who is usually punctual to every meeting arrives 20 minutes late one day and seems flustered. You immediately think, "Something serious must have happened on the way." According to the attribution logic, which pattern of information is most likely driving your judgement?
options: Low distinctiveness, high consensus, low consistency
High distinctiveness, low consensus, high consistency
High distinctiveness, low consensus, low consistency
Low distinctiveness, low consensus, high consistency
questions.explanation: External attribution occurs when the behavior is unusual for the person (high distinctiveness), others are not late (low consensus), and it is not their regular habit (low consistency).

questions.text: A team is trained on the distinctiveness-consensus-consistency framework. One member claims: "As long as consistency is high, we should always treat behaviour as caused by the individual." Based on the explanation given, what is the best critique of this statement?
options: It is correct beacuse consistency alone determines attribution
It is incorrect because high consistency must always mean an external cause
It is irrelevant because attribution does not use any of these three elements
It is incomplete because consistency must be interpreted together with distinctivness and consensus to decide between internal and external causes
questions.explanation: Attribution requires analyzing all three factors (consistency, distinctiveness, and consensus) together to accurately determine the cause of behavior.

questions.text: Which statement best captures the distinction between content and process theories of motivation?
options: Content theories explain how motivation operates over time; process theories list types of needs
Content theories ask “what” motivates people (needs, motives), whereas process theories ask “how” motivation occurs (the mechanisms and sequences)
Both content and process theories only study salary as a motivator
Process theories ignore behaviour and focus on personality
questions.explanation: Content theories focus on identifying the specific factors or needs that motivate individuals, while process theories examine the cognitive mechanisms behind the motivation.

questions.text: Which of the following aligns most closely with Alderfer’s modification of Maslow’s hierarchy?
options: Needs must always be satisfied strictly from physiological to self-actualisation without overlap
Only growth needs matter in organisations; existence and relatedness are irrelevant
Regression in needs is impossible once growth has begun
Needs are collapsed into existence, relatedness, and growth, can operate simultaneously, and people may regress when frustrated
questions.explanation: Alderfer's ERG theory simplifies needs and acknowledges that multiple needs can be active at once and individuals can move backwards (frustration-regression).

questions.text: A large multinational offers excellent pay, security, and facilities, yet new graduates often leave within a year, saying, “The work is boring; I don’t feel I’m growing.” Which integrated conclusion best explains this pattern?
options: Hygiene factors like salary and conditions eliminate dissatisfaction but do not by themselves create strong, sustained motivation
High hygiene automatically guarantees long-term engagement
Content theories ignore such situations
Only process theories can explain early attrition
questions.explanation: Based on Herzberg's theory, environmental factors (hygiene) prevent dissatisfaction but only motivators like growth and challenging work lead to job satisfaction.

questions.text: A high-performing employee believes she deserved a promotion but was passed over. After reviewing the talent-identification process, she concludes that multiple leaders applied clear, consistent criteria and that some colleagues matched those criteria more closely this year. She still feels disappointed but says, “The system is fair; I just need to improve.” Which outcome does this reaction exemplify?
options: Lack of distributive justice and system satisfaction
Strong system satisfaction from procedural justice, despite limited distributive justice
Breakdown of interactional justice
Complete loss of trust in the organisation
questions.explanation: Even though the outcome was unfavorable (low distributive justice), the employee accepts it because she views the decision-making process as transparent and fair (high procedural justice).

questions.text: According to Vroom’s model, which motivation statement is most accurate?
options: High motivation requires either expectancy or valence to be high, but not both
Motivation is additive: expectancy + instrumentality + valence
Motivation is multiplicative, so if any one of expectancy, instrumentality or valence is effectively zero, overall motivation collapses
Motivation depends only on valence, not on beliefs about effort or performance
questions.explanation: Vroom's Expectancy Theory posits that motivation is a product of three variables; if any variable is absent, total motivation is zero.

questions.text: A startup founder wants to “motivate” employees and is torn between increasing salaries slightly or redesigning roles to include more autonomy, client ownership, feedback, and visible impact. Using the closing link between content and process theories in the lecture, which reasoning is strongest?
options: Only salary changes matter: job design has no motivational effect

Job redesign that enriches responsibility and feedback can activate intrinsic motives (content side), while well-structured rewards and recognition systems apply process theories like expectancy and reinforcement
Extrinsic rewards make process theories unnecessary
Content theories and process theories should never be used together
questions.explanation: Combining job enrichment with fair rewards addresses both the 'what' (intrinsic needs) and 'how' (reinforcement mechanics) of employee motivation.

questions.text: Which accurately distinguishes programmed from non-programmed and strategic from operational decisions?
options: Programmed = routine/repetitive/rule-based (e.g., weekly reordering); non-programmed = unique/new (e.g., new market entry); strategic = long-term impact (e.g., geographic expansion); operational = daily execution (e.g., staff scheduling)
Programmed = unique/unstructured; strategic = day-to-day execution
Strategic always requires more time than operational
Non-programmed decisions have no trade-offs
questions.explanation: This distinction separates decisions based on their frequency/predictability (programmed vs non-programmed) and their level of impact/timeline (strategic vs operational).

questions.text: Which accurately captures the core characteristics and trade-offs of System 1 vs. System 2 thinking?
options: System 1 = deliberate/slow/accurate but effortful; System 2 = automatic/quick/intuitive
Both systems are always equally effortful
System 1 = automatic/intuitive/quick (e.g., recognising a friend) but risks snap errors; System 2 = deliberate/analytical/effortful (e.g., market entry) but more accurate
System 2 operates only for programmed decisions
questions.explanation: System 1 is fast and reflexive, useful for immediate tasks but prone to bias, while System 2 is slow and logical, necessary for complex problem-solving.

questions.text: You're interviewing candidates and notice left-handers like Da Vinci and Hendrix; you prefer them, assuming creativity. A colleague has challenges with right-handed creators. What bias explains your preference, and how should you counter it per the guidance?
options: Self-serving bias; ignore the challenge
Halo effect; distorts perceptions
Anchoring: change your reference group
Confirmation bias (favouring info confirming preconceptions); actively seek disconfirming evidence
questions.explanation: The preference results from confirmation bias, which can be mitigated by intentionally looking for evidence that contradicts one's preconceived notions.

questions.text: Which accurately summarises the intuitive method for predicting others' behaviour?
options: Observe phenomena → ignore data → guess randomly → predict
Observe phenomena → collect data → identify patterns → build heuristics → predict future behaviour
Focus only on current mood → immediate reaction
Avoid past experiences entirely
questions.explanation: Intuition in predicting behavior relies on systematic observation and the development of mental shortcuts (heuristics) based on past patterns.

questions.text: Which accurately captures the layered personality metaphor and its progression?
options: Outermost = behaviours → attitudes/beliefs → values (deeper layers), like peeling an onion to reveal core drivers
Outermost = values → attitudes/beliefs → behaviours (deepest)
All layers are equal and interchangeable
The iceberg metaphor shows behaviours as the largest visible part
questions.explanation: The onion metaphor suggests that visible behaviors are driven by progressively deeper and more stable layers of attitudes and core values.

questions.text: You misjudge a colleague's firm tone as rudeness, later learning it stemmed from repeated project failures. Integrating the onion metaphor and bias warning, what holistic takeaway guides future people management?
options: Surface behaviours alone suffice for feedback
Never give feedback on behaviours
All judgments are permanent
Behaviours (outer layer) trigger intuitive biases; peel to attitudes/context for accurate diagnosis, self-reflection, and adjusted engagement
questions.explanation: Management requires looking beyond surface-level actions to understand underlying motivations and context to avoid inaccurate snap judgments.

questions.text: You judge a colleague "difficult to work with" after one interaction, leading to avoidance. Reflecting, you realise this judgment shapes future engagements. Per the discussion, what process perpetuates this, and how should you counter it?
options: Temporary behaviour; ignore it
Attitude formation (judgment about a person) reinforced by selective info supporting the belief; seek disconfirming evidence and context
Superstition; use nudges
Values conflict; change their role
questions.explanation: Negative attitudes are often sustained by focusing only on information that confirms them; managers should actively seek contradictory evidence to break this cycle.

questions.text: According to the onion/iceberg logic, how do values relate to attitudes/beliefs and behaviours?
options: Values are outermost; behaviours are deepest
Values and behaviours are identical
Attitudes determine values, not the other way around
Values are a core layer; they shape enduring attitudes/beliefs, which in turn manifest in visible behaviours
questions.explanation: Values act as the fundamental core that dictates how an individual perceives the world (attitudes) and subsequently acts (behaviors).

questions.text: An employee says, “My pay is low,” and feels upset that friends earn more, yet does not apply elsewhere and continues to work. Which interpretation fits the three-component attitude model?
options: cognition (“pay is low”) and affect (“friends get more”) signal dissatisfaction, but the behavioural intention to act (quit/search) is not yet aligned
cognitive, affective, and behavioural components are fully aligned
only behaviour matters; thoughts and feelings are irrelevant
There is no attitude here, only behaviour
questions.explanation: This illustrates a case where the thinking (cognition) and feeling (affect) components of an attitude do not yet match the final action (behavior).

questions.text: According to the model, which sequence correctly describes the three components of an attitude?
options: behavioural (what I do) → affective (how I feel) → cognitive (what I think)
cognitive (opinions/beliefs) → affective (feelings/emotions) → behavioural (intention/action)
affective → behavioral only
cognitive only
questions.explanation: Attitudes typically form through a progression from a belief or thought to an emotional response, leading to a specific intention or action.

questions.text: A 17-year-old describes their weekly life as: school, playing for a local football club, volunteering at a temple, and visiting a swimming pool-run academy. Based on the framing used, which is the most accurate interpretation of their context?
options: They are surrounded only by government institutions
They are continuously interacting with multiple organisations that create products/services and structure activities
Only their school truly counts as an organisation
Organisations are limited to profit-making companies
questions.explanation: Everyday life involves constant interaction with various structured entities, whether educational, social, or recreational, which are all organizations.

questions.text: Which definition best reflects what an organisation is?
options: A random collection of people in one place
Any group of friends meeting socially
Only entities created for profit
A deliberate arrangement where tasks are grouped and delegated to people with relevant specialisation, with roles, authority and responsibilities coordinated to achieve a shared purpose
questions.explanation: An organization is defined by its intentional structure and the coordination of specialized roles to reach a specific common objective.

questions.text: You are mapping stakeholders for Domino’s, but list only “employees” and “customers.” What is the most substantial improvement to make this map more useful for people, work, and organisational decisions?
options: Remove customers and keep employees only
Differentiate internal roles (e.g., marketing, sales, delivery, production, managers) and add external actors (government, competitors, financiers) because their stakes and expectations differ
Focus only on government regulations
Include only shareholders as a generic category
questions.explanation: Effective stakeholder mapping requires identifying specific internal subgroups and external entities whose diverse interests impact the organization.

questions.text: You manage a local Domino’s outlet on a rainy Saturday evening. Online orders spike, walk-ins increase, and you must still uphold the 30-minute delivery promise. From the description of Domino’s operations, which managerial focus is most critical at this moment?
options: Coordinating tasks (order-taking, baking, dispatch, stock use) so that product, process and service standards set by the franchiser are maintained despite demand variability
Relying only on the headquarters’ brand image
Ignoring online orders to prioritise walk-ins
Stopping deliveries until the rain clears
questions.explanation: In high-demand periods, the manager's primary role is to coordinate various operational functions to ensure quality and service standards are met.

questions.text: Which description best captures Domino’s India in organisational terms?
options: A small local eatery with no formal structure
An informal group of delivery riders
A charity with no commercial objectives
A complex, large quick-service restaurant chain run in India by Jubilant FoodWorks under a franchise arrangement from the US parent
questions.explanation: Domino's India is a large-scale, formal corporate entity operating under a franchise model, involving complex legal and operational structures.

questions.text: You reflect on your neighbourhood gym using the input–conversion–output framework. Which analysis fits the description given?
options: Only the rent paid by the gym owner matters
Inputs (equipment, trainers, space) → conversion (trainer routines, scheduling, member support) → output (fitness experience and results) that you value enough to stay loyal
Value is created solely by advertisements, not by service
There are no identifiable inputs or outputs
questions.explanation: The gym adds value by transforming physical and human resources (inputs) into a beneficial fitness experience (output) through organized processes.

questions.text: Why is “customer loyalty and satisfaction” highlighted when discussing organisational survival and growth?
options: Because loyal customers signal that the organisation’s value creation (through people, processes and outputs) is actually perceived and appreciated
Because inputs are unimportant
Because marketing alone drives all success
Because regulations demand loyalty
questions.explanation: Customer loyalty validates that an organization is successfully creating value, which is essential for long-term sustainability.

questions.text: Which definition best captures a “stakeholder” in the Domino’s example?
options: Any person who buys pizza once
Only shareholders listed on stock exchanges
Only senior executives
Anyone who has a stake in the organisation’s actions and outcomes
questions.explanation: Stakeholders are all individuals or groups who can affect or are affected by the company's activities, including staff, customers, and regulators.

questions.text: You are advising a new QSR chain. They plan to focus solely on customer discounts and ignore complaints in consumer forums, assuming “only current buyers matter.” Using the Domino’s example, what is the strongest argument against this approach?
options: Unresolved complaints can escalate to legal/consumer bodies, turning courts, forums and the broader community into influential stakeholders whose negative perceptions can erode legitimacy and future demand
Discounts eliminate all reputational risk
Forums and courts cannot affect perception
Non-customers will never influence brand outcomes
questions.explanation: Ignoring grievances can turn passive groups into active, hostile stakeholders, damaging the company's long-term reputation and legal standing.
