questions.text: Domino’s India offers localised vegetarian and spicy items while promising consistent 30-minute delivery across ~1,900+ outlets and a strong online/app presence. For students of people, work and organisation, what integrated organisational capability does this best illustrate?
options: Pure dependence on technology alone
Lack of process because of franchising

Coordinated planning, organising and control across functions (procurement, kitchen, delivery, tech, marketing) to handle scale, variability in demand, and local preferences under a unified service promise
questions.explanation: Success at scale requires integrating diverse departments and processes to deliver a consistent customer experience despite regional variations.

questions.text: A member of the Jubilant FoodWorks board learns of a proposal to expand Domino’s into a new region. They focus on long-term profitability, regulatory compliance, and overall sustainability, but do not design the store-level operations. Which description best fits their role?
options: Operational manager responsible for day-to-day execution
Board member exercising oversight on behalf of shareholders over compliance and long term sustainability/profitability
Shareholder with no responsibilities
Delivery staff coordinating orders
questions.explanation: Board members focus on high-level strategy and governance to protect shareholder interests, rather than managing daily shop-floor tasks.

questions.text: You observe a manager who spends time clarifying team targets, arranging training, giving performance feedback, negotiating deadlines, and assigning ownership of key tasks. Which integrated description best fits their core responsibilities?
options: Ensuring individual technical excellence only
Avoiding involvement in people's issues
Focusing only on cost-cutting
Aligning the team with organisational goals, building people’s ability to perform productively, guiding them through feedback, and ensuring deadlines are met via effective delegation
questions.explanation: Effective managers balance the need for results with developing their team's skills and ensuring their work aligns with broader company objectives.

questions.text: Which set best summarises the four broad activities managers are responsible for?
options: Planning, marketing, accounting, and selling
Aligning the team to organisational goals; ensuring people/processes are productive; giving performance and development feedback; ensuring deadlines are met while employees grow their careers
Hiring, firing, budgeting, and reporting
Supervising attendance only
questions.explanation: Management involves setting direction, optimizing operations, developing people, and ensuring the timely delivery of results.

questions.text: Why is delegation called a “hallmark” of being an effective manager?
options: It lets managers escape accountability
It eliminates the need for feedback
It enables managers to get work done through others, distribute responsibilities, and support employees’ growth and development
It reduces the importance of team goals
questions.explanation: Delegation allows managers to scale their impact by empowering others to take on tasks, which simultaneously builds team capability.

questions.text: Two managers have the same technical background. Manager A focuses on her own deliverables; Manager B spends substantial time aligning team goals, training team members, providing feedback, resolving conflicts, and coordinating across functions. Which is more likely to be considered an effective manager, and why?
options: Manager A, because technical work is all that matters
Manager B, because effective management is about coordinating people and processes, getting results through others, and building cooperation and collaboration toward organisational purpose
Both equally, as roles are identical
Neither, because only senior leadership manages
questions.explanation: True management effectiveness comes from managing collective performance and interpersonal dynamics rather than focusing on individual output.

questions.text: You are newly promoted first-line manager in a Domino’s store. Most of your day is spent guiding team members in baking, packing and serving, dealing with customers, and coordinating with riders. Which skill mix is most critical at this level?
options: Conceptual skills only
Technical and interpersonal skills, because you must do the job well and work closely with people and customers
Diagnostic skills only
Legal and lobbying skills
questions.explanation: First-line managers need technical knowledge to supervise operations and strong interpersonal skills to interact directly with staff and customers.

questions.text: You move from a store manager role to a regional head overseeing 25 outlets. You now rarely bake pizzas yourself but must anticipate operational bottlenecks, interpret market changes and support outlet managers in solving complex problems. How does the relative importance of your skills shift?
options: Technical skills increase; diagnostic and conceptual skills decrease
Interpersonal skills disappear
Diagnostic and conceptual skills become more central, while technical details can be obtained from others
Only technical skills matter
questions.explanation: As managers rise in hierarchy, they rely more on analytical and strategic thinking (conceptual) while delegating specific technical tasks.

questions.text: Which definition of work is correct?
options: Any activity done only for money
Effortful, productive activity that results in a product or service
Only tasks assigned by a formal employer
Activities that others approve of
questions.explanation: Work is broadly defined as any purposeful effort that creates value or useful outcomes, regardless of whether it is paid.

questions.text: How is “job” distinguished from “work”?
options: Work is any effortful productive activity; a job is work performed in a formal organisational context where specific tasks/activities are assigned with expectations toward organisational outcomes (often monetised)
Job refers only to creative professions
Work must be unpaid; job must be paid
Job and work are exact synonyms
questions.explanation: While 'work' is a general activity, a 'job' refers specifically to a formalized role within an organization with specific duties and rewards.

questions.text: Younger people are increasingly drawn to hobbies and entrepreneurial projects that give them personal and social meaning, while families may not see these as “real work.” What is the most helpful way to interpret this tension?
options: Younger people reject the concept of work
Only the elders’ definition of work is valid
Meaning has no role in how we define work
It reflects differing mental models and meanings of work (beyond traditional jobs), requiring explicit reflection and dialogue about how various activities meet the core definition
questions.explanation: Societal perceptions of work evolve; understanding these different perspectives requires communication about value and purpose beyond traditional employment.

questions.text: You are a newly promoted team lead with five direct reports. During a weekly check-in, one employee shares their career aspirations while struggling with a deadline. According to the expanded role of managers beyond traditional tasks, your most comprehensive response would involve:
options: Delegating the deadline task to another team member without discussion.
Reporting the issue to HR for staffing adjustments.
Providing one-on-one coaching, feedback, and guidance on career goals alongside task coordination.
Budgeting extra hours for the employee to complete the work alone.
questions.explanation: Modern management involves balancing operational task delivery with personal coaching and career development for team members.

questions.text: As a department head, your day starts with a supplier dispute, shifts to a team crisis over missed deliverables, includes a peer meeting, and ends with a customer call-all fragmented and fast-paced. This routine primarily highlights manager's:
options: Preference for written reports over oral exchanges.
Heavy involvement in crisis management, conflict resolution, and quick context switches.
Slow, reflective planning without interruptions.
Exclusive focus on direct reports, avoiding external contacts.
questions.explanation: Managerial work is characterized by high variety, fragmentation, and brevity, requiring frequent shifts between different types of urgent problems.

questions.text: As a manager, you notice your calendar is packed with appointments, leading to constant firefighting after minor issues escalate unnoticed, then reverting to routine busyness. This cycle matches the description of:
options: Managing on autopilot, creating false security where problems fester, explode, and pull you into reactive mode.
Mindful reflection, breaking the vicious autopilot loop.
Balanced communication limited to vertical interactions.
Low-pressure work with ample time for reading and writing.
questions.explanation: This describes the 'activity trap' where managers become so busy with routines that they lose proactive awareness and only react to crises.

questions.text: You observe one manager resolving team conflicts proactively with feedback while another escalates them, both using similar situations but differing in approach, achieving equal effectiveness through personal strengths. This scenario demonstrates people management as:
options: Pure science with reproducible, uniform outcomes regardless of personality.
Common sense needs no attention or training.
An art excluding enduring rules or hypotheses.
A craft blending science principles and individual artistry for signature styles.
questions.explanation: Management is a craft because it uses established principles (science) but requires individual judgment and personal style (art) for execution.

questions.text: Amid activity traps where tasks dominate, managers forget to ask how they add value to themselves, underscoring that without mindfulness, they overlook people management's foundation as:
options: Unimportant compared to technical training.
Simplistic common sense everyone possesses naturally.
An unintended outcome of daily activities alone.
A science with rules, hypotheses, and principles like delegation applicable across contexts.
questions.explanation: Mindful management recognizes that while it requires personal style, it is grounded in systematic rules and principles that can be studied and applied.

questions.text: Studies on great managers, including Google's Project Oxygen, reveal that beyond varied "laundry lists," effective leaders share:
options: Unique traits varying by organisation without overlap.
Attributes needing five-point or more detailed rating scales.
Core characteristics corroborated across multiple research studies.
Qualities assessed only through other's feedback, not self-reflection.
questions.explanation: Research across various organizations consistently identifies a shared set of essential management behaviors that drive high performance.

questions.text: As a software services manager surveyed by NASCOM in 2016-17, you note rising AI automation, young employee aspirations, and competitor collaborations changing hiring and strategy. These findings confirm that external changes consistently impact:
options: Managers through varying intensities across all organisations.
Isolated sectors without cross-industry patterns.
Uniform effects regardless of nation or business type.
Only employee demographics without regulatory influence.
questions.explanation: Macro-environmental shifts in technology and society force managers in all organizations to adapt their strategies and people practices.

questions.text: Over the last 5 years, your organisation has shifted from siloed departments chasing fixed targets to cross-team collaboration and quick experimentation on new delivery models amid regulatory changes. As a manager, this requires you to:
options: Foster collaboration, innovation, and agile planning with multiple contingency options.
Maintain autopilot execution without revisiting goals.
Enforce stricter departmental isolation for efficiency.
Prioritise routinization over environmental anticipation.
questions.explanation: Changing environments require managers to abandon rigid structures in favor of flexible, collaborative work styles and proactive planning.

questions.text: Innovation as challenging status quo- improving what works, fixing what doesn't pairs with essential skills like deep listening to access:
options: Multiple perspectives for informed decisions and alternative ideas.
Dismissal of challenging viewpoints.
Fear-free participation stifled by biases.
Single hierarchical viewpoint dominance.
questions.explanation: Innovation requires managers to listen deeply to various viewpoints to find better ways to operate and avoid stagnant decision-making.

questions.text: As a first-time technical manager, you fear delegating a strategy proposal, fearing replacement, but holding it plateaus your growth while freeing time for higher priorities if delegated right. Effective delegation requires:
options: Retaining all competence to avoid direct reports excelling.
Explaining only under non-tight deadlines.
Trust, ownership transfer, and continuous personal growth post-delegation.
Walking away without support or clarity.
questions.explanation: Success in delegation comes from trusting employees with real responsibility while the manager focuses on developing new strategic capabilities.

questions.text: Managers commonly err by delegating tasks they dislike, creating a culture where reports mimic avoidance, undermining:
options: 30-40% enjoyable job portions only.
Expertise building for threshold competence.
Tight-deadline expert handoffs.
Discipline of walking the talk despite unliked duties.
questions.explanation: Managers must lead by example; dumping only undesirable tasks on subordinates sends a negative message about work ethic and professional standards.

questions.text: Delegation succeeds by providing six clarities, starting with expected outcomes and including responsibility, time milestones, priority tied to a larger vision, communication with support queries, and:
options: Task subsets without accountability.
Consequences covering personal and organisational rewards/impacts.
Personal biases during handoff.
Micromanagement post-assignment.
questions.explanation: The final part of successful delegation is ensuring the employee understands the positive or negative impact of the task's completion.

questions.text: Your expert team member excels at competition analysis but holds no decision authority in the bureaucratic structure, while a newcomer requests more responsibility. Optimal delegation balances:
options: Immediate task completion, ignoring positional constraints.
Expertise with authority position and growth opportunities for motivated members.
Uniform assignment without sensitivity assessment.
Repeated expert reliance despite plateauing risks.
questions.explanation: Effective delegation matches tasks to an individual's skill level while providing growth opportunities to keep team members motivated.

questions.text: Your team meets numbers, but internal stakeholders complain about service quality despite repeated clarifications. To help them see the bigger picture, you delegate a team member to:
options: Study processes, benchmark other departments, and gather stakeholder feedback.
Reorganise immediately without external exposure.
Assign individual activity fixes without socialisation.
Micromanage quality metrics directly.
questions.explanation: Delegating cross-functional investigation helps employees understand external expectations and improves overall service quality and internal alignment.

questions.text: After mapping stakeholders with yourself at the centre, you realise that beyond direct reports, your manager's peer, cross-department peers, and suppliers influence your project success. This exercise reveals:
options: Only direct reports qualify as true stakeholders.
External groups like customers have no internal relevance.
Stakeholders are people with stakes in your success and whose success you impact.
Skip-level managers lack expectations from you.
questions.explanation: Stakeholder mapping demonstrates that a manager's effectiveness depends on managing a web of mutual dependencies beyond just their direct team.

questions.text: Cross-functional conflicts between your direct reports and peer's team escalate despite numbers being met, requiring you to avoid blame games and solve them jointly. Practical peer management demands:
options: Enforcing departmental silos for clarity.
Understanding pain points and mitigating them beyond task focus.
Competitor framing for resource protection.
Withholding credit to maintain leverage.
questions.explanation: Managing peers involves looking beyond immediate tasks to understand shared challenges and finding collaborative solutions to systemic conflicts.

questions.text: Promoted over peer applicants to manage them, you face awkward dynamics, wondering how to shift from hanging out to leading. The critical first preparation step is:
options: Strong conversation with your manager for clarity on expectations and goals.
Continuing social hangouts to ease tension.
Immediate authority assertion in group settings.
Delegating all decisions, avoiding direct involvement.
questions.explanation: When transitioning to leading former peers, the manager must first align with their own supervisor to establish professional boundaries and role clarity.

questions.text: Your best friend, now a direct report, receives extra responsibilities and sensitive info you once shared casually as peers. To maintain professional boundaries:
options: Continue gossiping for relationship preservation.
Avoid favouritism, confidential sharing, and badmouthing others.
Give them all strategic tasks due to friendship trust.
Maintain identical casual interactions post-promotion.
questions.explanation: Professionalism requires setting clear boundaries to prevent personal relationships from creating perceptions of bias or confidentiality breaches in the workplace.

questions.text: Your intuitive, open-door boss is replaced by a formal, documentation-heavy leader who demands agendas. Adapting from morning walk-ins to structured emails reveals the key to success:
options: Knowing your boss's preferred information style and work methods.
Maintaining identical interaction patterns.
Complaining about the unavailability to colleagues.
Escalating access issues to HR.
questions.explanation: 'Managing up' involves identifying and adapting to a supervisor's specific communication preferences and operational needs.

questions.text: Effective boss management patterns emphasise knowing priorities, strengths/weaknesses, info preferences, aspirations, plus:
options: Throwing surprises for attention.
Independent decision-making isolation.
Problem dumping without solutions.
Flagging issues with pre-emptive solutions from the boss's perspective.
questions.explanation: A proactive manager supports their superior by identifying potential problems early and suggesting viable solutions from a higher-level perspective.

questions.text: A newly appointed manager wants to quickly understand employee's views on a recent policy change and collect their ideas for improvement. Which option best represents an appropriate primary channel for this purpose?
options: Circulating the updated standard operating manuals.
Conducting an employee feedback survey or using suggestion boxes to gather inputs.
Publishing a detailed article in the in-house magazine.
Asking senior leaders to send a top-down email about the policy.
questions.explanation: Feedback channels like surveys and suggestion boxes are designed to facilitate two-way communication and collect honest employee input efficiently.

questions.text: Which option best captures the core roles for the sender and receiver in the communication process?
options: The sender hears and interprets, while the receiver only thinks and transmits.
Both sender and receiver perform identical fuctions without any distinction.
The sender thinks and communicates ideas using language, while the receiver hears and interpretes the message through their own perceptions and experiences.
The sender only provides written messages and the.
questions.explanation: Communication is an active process of encoding by the sender and decoding by the receiver, often influenced by subjective backgrounds.

questions.text: A new manager from HR attends a supply chain review where colleagues constantly use terms like “SAP”, “JIT”, and other internal codes. The manager leaves confused, even though everyone else found the discussion clear and logical. Which option best explains why communication failed for this manager?
options: The message relied on jargon meaningful only within that specific community, making the message difficult for an outsider to understand.
The sender's role alone determines effectiveness, so the receiver's background is irrelevant.
The medium of a face-to-face meeting always guarantees complete understanding for all participants.
The presence if psychological noise is impossible in formal organisational settings.
questions.explanation: Semantic barriers like industry-specific jargon can exclude those who are not part of that specialized group from understanding the core message.

questions.text: A manager has two reliable direct reports who always execute tasks well and listen attentively, making them favourites. When a colleague points out minor error in their recent work, the manager brushes them of without investiagtion. Which explantion fits this scenario?
options: A information overload from managing too many tasks at once.
Selective perception causes the manager not to see any bad in those favourites, even when highlighted.
A language barrier between the colleague and the manager.
Lack of self-awareness about the manager's own biases.
questions.explanation: Selective perception leads people to ignore or rationalize negative information about individuals they favor, maintaining their internal bias.

questions.text: What two linked dimensions are highlighted for language as a barrier?
options: Only fluency in one's native language.
Stereotypes alone without cultural context.
Technology use in local terms.
Lack of proficiency in a common language like English, combined with cultural differences across regions and nations.
questions.explanation: Language barriers in organizations are often a mix of technical proficiency gaps and deeper cultural differences in communication styles.

questions.text: Which option correctly outlines the progresssion triggered by communication barriers?
options: Confusion → misunderstanding → defensiveness → mistrust → conflict
Mistrust → defensiveness → confusion → conflict → misunderstanding
Misunderstanding → confusion → defensiveness → mistrust → conflict
Defensiveness → misunderstanding → mistrust → confusion → conflict
questions.explanation: Unresolved communication failures typically escalate from initial confusion into interpersonal defensiveness and long-term organizational conflict.

questions.text: A top individual contributor on the team expects promotion soon after a year of gentle coaching, but the manager sees no managerial potential. The manager avoids the talk, fearing it will damage morale or prompt resginations. What defines this as difficult?
options: It induces no stress, just a simple affirmation.
Anxiety over phrasing without hurting, uncertain reception, and consequences like turnover.
Only relevant for bossess, not team members.
Easy because the employee is reliable.
questions.explanation: Difficult conversations are defined by high emotional stakes, differing perspectives, and potentially significant negative outcomes if handled poorly.

questions.text: A manager's star team member consistently hits targets but is rude and withholds information from colleagues, eroding team dynamics despite being a "calling". Into which category does this fall?
options: Performance-related, specifically objective high performance with attitudinal issues.
Interpersonal conflict between manager and team.
Job skills plateau unrelated to behaviour.
Organisational policy enforcement.
questions.explanation: This describes a 'performance-attitude' gap where excellent technical output is marred by poor teamwork and destructive interpersonal behavior.

questions.text: How does preparation for challenging conversation differ from regular meetings?
options: No preparation needed due to anxiety.
Less focus, same as performance reviews.
Greater emphasis, comprehensiveness: know objective/outcome, problem from both views, open mind for listening/solutions.
Only document policy, ignore employees view.
questions.explanation: Preparation for tough talks requires deep empathy and planning to ensure the manager remains balanced and solution-oriented during high stress.

questions.text: A manager prepares to inform a non-performer of a new organisational layoff policy, spending 30-45 minutes documenting. What must guide the objective and outcome?
options: Objective: communicate policy change and impact; outcome: employee understands potential exit if no improvement, preparing to seek a new job.
Objective: debate policy fairness; outcome: employee quits immediately.
Objective: coach behaviour only; outcome: instant promotion.
Objective: Avoid all mention of performance.
questions.explanation: Clarity in both the factual message (objective) and the desired understanding or action (outcome) is essential for effective communication of bad news.

questions.text: What singular role must a manager anchor in?
options: Friend or coach giving casual advice.
Peer sharing ideas informally.
Manager only, with organisational responsibilities, behaving accordingly (250% clear).
Mentor focused on long-term growth alone.
questions.explanation: Despite being friendly or coaching, a manager's primary identity must remain as a formal representative of the organization's goals and policies.

questions.text: Which sequence covers key conduct?
options: Talk more, avoid silence, and provide support.
Hide objective, use "we" always.
Play victim for popularity.
State objective/agenda upfront, talk less/listen more (incl. unsaid), summarise end, mindful "I" (feel/think/suggest), stick facts/purpose.
questions.explanation: Effective communication conduct involves setting clear expectations, active listening, and using 'I' statements to own one's perspective.

questions.text: A manager wants extra hours for project recognition, but family urges to park in the evening. Tension builds over differing needs. Which conflict type?
options: Interpersonal: disagreement between the individual’s work ideas and the family’s priorities/thoughts.
Only intrapersonal self-doubt.
Intergroup team vs. home.
No conflict, just scheduling.
questions.explanation: This is interpersonal conflict where the needs and expectations of one party (family) clash with those of another (the individual's work goals).

questions.text: Manager reflects on a bitter, unresolved friend conflict that has persisted for years vs. a resolved sibling one. Insight for teams?
options: “Conflicts always disabling; avoid.”
“Analyse resolved (parties/issue/resolution) vs. unresolved (lingering bad taste)—perceive negative impacts early for healthy handling.”
“Only intrapersonal matter.”
“No reflection needed; inevitable bitterness.”
questions.explanation: Analyzing how past conflicts were resolved helps teams proactively manage future disagreements before they become toxic and permanent.

questions.text: How do dyadic/intragroup/intergroup variations affect managers' conflict resolution styles?
options: Identical mediation role always.
No variation needed.
Vary manager mediation role: direct for dyadic, facilitate intragroup, and different for intergroup.
Only the intergroup requires action.
questions.explanation: Conflict resolution strategies must adapt depending on whether the issue is between two individuals, within a single team, or across different departments.

questions.text: How should a manager respond when a senior snaps, "Please don't speak," to silence a junior's valid input during a meeting?
options: Apologise, assuming a misunderstanding.
Ignore as standard hierarchy.
Let it escalate to voting.
Intervene immediately as an assertive verbal attack to protect speaking rights and credibility.
questions.explanation: A manager must act as a protector of inclusive communication, assertively addressing attempts to silence team members regardless of seniority.

questions.text: What is the core reason managers exist in conflict situations?
options: To resolve right vs. wrong via algorithms.
To avoid stress by delegating.
To decide between right and more right from the bigger organisational picture beyond conflicting parties.
To ensure both sides are fully satisfied.
questions.explanation: Managerial intervention in conflict aims to balance competing interests in a way that best serves the organization's overarching goals.

questions.text: Two peer departments under your purview conflict, but they have no direct impact on your operations. What tactical approach preserves your relationships with both?
options: Intervene with a superordinate goal emphasising organisational aims.
Avoid involvement and let them resolve it independently.
Compromise by allocating shared resources equally.
Educate them on each other's perspectives in a joint session.
questions.explanation: When conflicts between peers do not affect one's own work, staying neutral (avoidance) can be a valid tactic to remain untainted by the dispute.

questions.text: Which conflict resolution method involves convening parties to define the problem, gather perspectives, and secure commitments for change?
options: Superordinate goal approach
Problem-solving orientation
Smoothing differences
Usage of authority
questions.explanation: The problem-solving orientation focuses on open communication and collaborative fact-finding to find a mutually beneficial solution.

questions.text: The founder daily ensures vision cascades to all, measures intangibles for viability despite unprofitability. Operations fixate on year-end targets. How does this contrast classify actions?
options: Manager outputs over owner outcomes
Owner long-term over manager short-term
Equal preoccupation balance
Profit trumps all sustainability
questions.explanation: Founders/owners typically focus on long-term enterprise health and mission, while managers often focus on immediate operational KPIs.

questions.text: Rapid growth triggers quick decisions in unfamiliar areas. Outcomes include upward escalation, overload, cracks, and collaboration suffers. What initiates this?
options: Ample prior experience
Stable job roles
Internal grooming pace
Need for rapid decision-making
questions.explanation: Scaling challenges often stem from the pressure to make fast choices in new contexts without established supporting structures.

questions.text: Firm doubles headcount in 4 years; few old-timers juggle interviews, decisions, ramp-up training. New HR department forms amid culture drift. What drives formalisation?
options: Surging selection/training demands
Reduced newcomer influx
Founder vision alone
questions.explanation: Growth necessitates the creation of formal HR processes to handle the volume of hiring and training that informal systems can no longer manage.

questions.text: Organisation design encompasses structure (hardware: tangible/visible) and culture (software: intangible/invisible). Analogy stresses compatibility for efficiency/effectiveness. What unifies both elements?
options: Culture secondary to charts
Structure always dominates
Mutual compatibility is essential
Independent optimisation suffices
questions.explanation: A firm's formal reporting lines (structure) and shared values (culture) must be aligned to function effectively as a whole.

questions.text: The entrepreneur dismisses organisation design as a mere "organisation chart," citing easy Microsoft Office templates from a Google search. How should a founder apply a deeper view amid scaling?
options: Rely solely on chart visuals
Integrate structure and culture
Ignore design entirely
Copy large firm templates
questions.explanation: Design involves more than just a chart; it requires intentional planning of how roles and cultural norms support the company's growth.

questions.text: No two organisations share culture, akin to unique personalities. Adjectives (supportive/caring) sound alike, yet experiences diverge. Why inevitable uniqueness?
options: Common descriptors unify
Shared across visits
Founder-independent norms
Distinct programming per group
questions.explanation: Every organization develops a unique 'social DNA' or programming based on its history, leadership, and group dynamics.

questions.text: A startup tech firm in Meerut loses its star software developer, who handled coding, client demos, and team mentoring. The CEO insists on hiring "another exactly like Raj" instead of redefining the role. What is the primary flaw in the CEO's approach?
options: Failing to recognise that a position exists independent of the person filling it, treating it as person-specific rather than task-based.
Overemphasising tasks while ignoring the unique skills Raj brought to demos.
Ignoring global benchmarks for software roles in startups.
Assuming all developers can handle mentoring without training.
questions.explanation: Organizational roles should be defined by necessary tasks and outputs, not by the unique combination of traits of a single former employee.

questions.text: A new edtech startup hires its first content creator. Students expect engaging tutorials (mentor role), investors want innovation reports (contributor role), and colleagues seek admin help. Yet the hiring doc lists only "produce 10 videos/week" without stakeholder expectations. How does this impact organisational structure?
options: Weakens the position as a building block by lacking clarity on roles/responsibilities from significant others, beyond easily measured tasks.
Prioritises self-expectations over stakeholder ones, like innovation.
Treats the role like theatre without a performing arts context.
Limits quantification to videos, ignoring admin tasks.
questions.explanation: A role that lacks clarity on how it serves various stakeholders creates performance gaps and weakens the organizational foundation.

questions.text: A growing Delhi e-commerce firm restructures amid rapid market shifts, reassigning logistics tasks across teams without founder oversight on position specs. This causes hiring mismatches, uneven performance evaluations, and salary disputes. Why is leadership involvement critical?
options: Delegation to admin for short-term profits.
Position descriptions as basis for restructuring (tasks/roles reassignment), ads, selection, compensation, and performance in volatile contexts.
Focus only on today's salaries, not 5-10 year talent.
Grouping functions without responsibility clarity.
questions.explanation: Leadership must ensure that role definitions are accurate and aligned with the company's new structure to maintain fairness and operational efficiency.

questions.text: An engineering startup skips technical coding tests for developers, using one inexperienced junior panel repeating "tell me about yourself" across two rounds. A fluent Excel-proficient candidate with strong tech skills is rejected for "not senior enough," despite culture fit, delaying projects. What process flaw persists?
options: Two rounds deemed excessive.
Juniors are ideal for screening.
Culture secondary to skills.
No role-tailored tests (technical fluency extent), untrained/non-senior panels lacking cultural grasp, repetitive questions amplifying biases.
questions.explanation: Ineffective selection processes that lack objective, role-relevant testing and trained interviewers lead to biased and poor hiring decisions.

questions.text: During 2025 economic volatility, a manufacturing founder opts out of sales manager panels for "strategic time," hiring via agencies. Culture becomes compliance-focused rather than adaptive, undermining the long-term enterprise legacy as an agile innovator. What parameter was undervalued?
options: Selection as key legacy driver, where founder interviews embed philosophy into the culture bedrock for high performance.
Agencies for efficiency.
Volatility excusing absence.
Sales not culture-core.
questions.explanation: A founder's personal involvement in key hires is vital for passing on organizational values and maintaining a specific performance culture.

questions.text: Sarah runs a successful organic export business. Initially, her goal was "maximum quarterly profit." However, after attending a leadership module, she decides to redefine her performance criteria. She begins focusing on building a "performance culture" and assesses whether the business can maintain its trajectory if she steps away to focus on long-term vision. If Sarah’s business continues to run "business as usual" in her absence, what does this imply about her role according to the four dimensions of performance management?
options: The enterprise has failed because the founder is no longer contributing to the sales turnover.
Sarah has successfully merged her personal success with the enterprise's success.
The enterprise is successful, and Sarah must now transition from managing "business as usual" to contributing to the enterprise's growth through a vision that is "beyond her."
The organisational performance management system is flawed because it lacks individual goal setting for the founder.
questions.explanation: Successful scaling means the company's daily operations no longer rely on the founder, allowing them to shift to high-level strategic growth.

questions.text: A company has high sales turnover and excellent profit margins, but its employees are frequently unable to implement innovative ideas shared by customers. According to the multi-dimensional criteria, where is the primary gap?
options: The Financial Perspective
The Organisation Dimension
The Customer Dimension
The Strategic Perspective
questions.explanation: Inability to implement ideas suggests a failure in internal organizational processes and structures despite financial success.

questions.text: A software company has a goal to "Improve Product Reliability." The CEO has told the Managers, but the junior programmers remain focused solely on "Coding Speed," resulting in many bugs. What is the fundamental failure here?
options: The company failed to cascade the "Reliability" dimension down to the programmers' specific tasks and behaviours.
The programmers are not talented enough to code quickly and reliably.
The CEO should not have shared the goal with the Managers first.
Coding speed is more important than product reliability in a performance culture.
questions.explanation: Strategy fails when top-level goals are not translated into specific, actionable objectives for individual employees at the operational level.

questions.text: Why is it a problem if an entrepreneur’s goal sheet is 80% "Business as Usual" (BAU)?
options: Because it means the entrepreneur is doing the work they hired managers to do.
Because BAU goals are not important for the organisation's survival.
Because BAU goals should only be assigned to the junior-most staff.
Because BAU goals don't have any financial impact.
questions.explanation: An entrepreneur should focus on growth and strategy; if they spend too much time on routine tasks, they are likely micro-managing or failing to lead.

questions.text: During a quarterly review, a lead developer admits she is struggling because the company recently switched to a new programming language she doesn't know. How should a leader applying this module's coaching framework respond?
options: Identify this as a "personal constraint" and use coaching to bridge the capability gap.
Treat it as a performance failure and reduce her "Business as Usual" weightage.
Ignore the issue and tell her to figure it out on her own time.
Re-assign her to a different department where the old language is still used.
questions.explanation: Effective performance management identifies skill gaps not as failures but as areas for coaching and developmental support.

questions.text: Why must entrepreneurs evaluate their lives holistically when considering legacy?
options: To compartmentalise business success from personal roles
To prioritise leaving a legacy over living one
To focus only on managerial and enterprise effectiveness
Because they embody multiple roles—entrepreneur, manager, owner, and family member (husband, wife, etc.)—requiring balanced assessment across effectiveness dimensions and well being
questions.explanation: A sustainable legacy requires the founder to be effective in all their life roles, ensuring that professional success does not come at the expense of personal health or family.

questions.text: A founder scaling their tech startup repeatedly shares the organisation's purpose, vision for sustainable growth beyond profitability, and core values in every team meeting—sometimes over 100 times—to inspire alignment, while humbly challenging team ideas with probing "why" questions, as if ignorant, without diminishing respect. When implementing new processes like timely reporting, the founder adheres first. This leadership style exemplifies:
options: Expecting all employees to think like the founder for efficiency intuitively
Limiting discussions to profitability goals without philosophical depth
Key dimensions of managerial effectiveness: communicating vision passionately, humbly challenging assumptions, and walking the talk to signal systems
Treating questioning as authoritative commands to enforce the founder's ideas
questions.explanation: Effective leaders drive alignment by constantly communicating the mission, being open to better ideas, and modeling expected behavior themselves.

questions.text: During a cash flow crisis, delaying salaries, an entrepreneur refrains from expressing frustration, instead regulating emotions to guide the team through solutions, maintaining positivity with "this too shall pass" while modelling resilience against banker pressures and project delays. This approach counters scaling pitfalls by:
options: Reinforcing team failures to motivate faster learning
Demonstrating personal effectiveness via emotion regulation, resilience under adversity, and enabling others rather than disabling them
Isolating personal emotions from organisational needs
Demanding immediate execution without acknowledging learning curves
questions.explanation: A leader's ability to remain calm and constructive during crises provides stability and keeps the team focused on solutions rather than panic.

questions.text: What is the primary dimension for entrepreneurs in leaving a legacy regarding organisational foundations?
options: Courageously, periodically re-examining philosophies, assumptions, beliefs—adapting to the environment while retaining the core to avoid extinction
Permanently fixing initial values without reclarification
Delegating the challenge to management teams exclusively
Ignoring external changes for internal consistency
questions.explanation: A lasting legacy requires an organization that can evolve its methods and beliefs to match a changing world while staying true to its essential purpose.

questions.text: In a large manufacturing firm, managers notice inconsistent employee behvaiours during peak production hours, leading to delays and safety issues and drawing from the idea that policies function like traffic signals to regulate behaviours in organisations with many employees, which approach would most effectively address this?
options: Implement strict HRM policies on work hours and safety protocols to standardise actions across all shifts.
Rely on individuals manager discretion to handle behaviour cases case-by-case without formal guidelines.
Ignore minor inconsistencies as they naturally resolve through peer pressure over time.
Focus solely on financial incentives without addressing behavioural regulation mechanisms
questions.explanation: HRM policies act like traffic signals to provide clear guidelines and standardise behavior across a large workforce, ensuring safety and consistency.

questions.text: Critically analyse Storey's 1995 definition of HRM as "a distinctive approach to employement management which seeks to achieve competitive advanatage through the strategic development of a highly committed and capable workforce using an integrated array of cultural, structural, and personnel techniques". Which element most directly challenges the exploitative connotation of "resource" by reframing people as human capital?
options: The emphais on employemnt management life cycles from joining to exit.
The requirement for a distinctive, organisation-specific implementation to build commitment and capability startegically.
The integrated use of cultural and structural techniques alognside personnel practices for holistic workforce deployment.
The sole focus on competitve advantage without reference to employee growth.
questions.explanation: Reframing people as human capital focuses on building commitment and capability through strategic, organization-specific development rather than treating them as mere exploitable resources.

questions.text: During a merger, a financial services firm struggles with low workforce capability as employees from both entities resist new cultural norms, leading to failed strategy execution despite capable hires. Reflecting on HRM as human capital management beyind exploitative views, eveluate how to achieve competitive advantage through workforce deployment, what integrated actions aligns organisation deisgn, structure, culture, and practices?
options: Maintain seperate policies for legacy employees to preserve familiarity.
Priorities structural changes only, deferring cultural and personnel integration.
Focus on termination for resistors rather than building commitment.

Leverage HRM policies as central to future thinking holistically combining cultural, structural, and personnel techniques for strategic deployment of a highly committed workforce across the employee life cycle to implement merger strategies effectively.
questions.explanation: Achieving competitive advantage during a merger requires a holistic integration of culture, structure, and personnel practices to build a committed workforce.

questions.text: "Are they transactions? Are they costs? Or are they value creators?" in the context of moving employees form "purely a cost" to contributing capital". Which evaluation principle from HRM's strategic essence best guides line managers in growing economies that require continuous innovation?
options: Categorise rigdly into cost or capital bins wihtout transformation efforts.
Treat uniformly as costs to optmise 70-80% wage burdens via reductions.

Cultivate as dynamic value creators by building commitment to organisational beliefs, harnessing engagement for innovation, and developing social capital through grooming and novel contributions that are appreciated organisationally.
View as transactional assests valued only for immediate output.
questions.explanation: In innovation-led economies, viewing and cultivating employees as value creators and intellectual capital is essential for long-term success.

questions.text: A retail chain purusing a cost-leadership business strategy experiences high absenteeism and low customer satisfaction scores, despite aggressive recruitment via social media, as selected employees lack motivation and fail to deliver efficient service or innovative ideas. Critically evaluate how refining HR strategies, distinct from conflating recruitment with selection, can align employee skills abilities, competencies, motivation, and engagement to drive human resource outcome like productively and quality, ultimately impacting financial performance.
options: Intensify recruitment efforts alone to attract more applicants without refining selection or performance management.
Ignore employee motivation, focusing only on operational metrics like reduced accidents.
Treate recruitment and selection synonymously, applying broad attention methods to all hires.

Develop integrated HR strategies (recruitment to attract qualified applicants, targeted selection to reject mismatches, performance management, training, compensation, and communication) to harness employee attributes for positive behaviours, yielding HR outcomes (efficiency, customer satisfaction) that balster business financial results.
questions.explanation: Integrated HR strategies ensure that individual attributes are aligned with organizational needs to drive productive behaviors and business impact.

questions.text: Evaluate the assertion: "Alignment enables HR outcomes. Misalignment disables HR outcomes," with line managers crucial in contributing to practices/processes and influencing policies/programs. What reflective principle guides assessoing organisational effectiveness?
options: Probe inconsistencies/strengths across 5Ps, as pilosophy-policy-program-practice-process cohesion (e.g, internal growth via feedback-influenced alignment) drives behaviours yielding HR outocmes (retention, skills), positively impacting business results thriugh HR-line-senior collaboration.
Ignore line manager feedback, centralising in the HR department.
View misalignment as inevitable without intervention.
Confine reflection to philosophy statement only.
questions.explanation: Organizational effectiveness is driven by the internal cohesion and alignment across the 5Ps (Philosophy, Policy, Program, Practice, Process).

questions.text: An architecture studio hiring for sustainble design roles uses face-to-face interviews where panels give candidates and hour to design nature-merging spaces. Still, poor conduct leaves applicants feeling undignified, leading to negative social media experiences that deter future talent amid the war for talent. Synthesise manager's culture-building via selection's four aspects and three C's to signal welcome behaviours and organisational values.
options: Ignore psychological context, focusing only on the physical setup.
Use hypothetical scenarios without face validity.

Align job description (tasks/responsibilities), specification (skills/attitudes), elimination via application attributes, innovation simulation (e.g. design tasks assessing creativity/aesthetics/sustainbility), structured open-ended questions from form notes, comfortable conduct (introduce, non-threatening start, candidate 2/3 talk time, no offense), ensuirng great experience as brand ambassadors signalling acceptable culture.
Treat interview as show-off oppotunities for interviewers.
questions.explanation: Selection is a culture-building process; managing the context, content, and conduct (3Cs) ensures candidates perceive the organization positively as brand ambassadors.

questions.text: Evaluate managers as brand ambassadors in talent wars, where poor candidated experience spreads negatively online. In contrast, great processes signal culture. What principle optimise selection for organisational signalling?
options: Basic rules: Introduce self, start comfortably (hobby/school open-ended), simulate a job (sell paper for sales attributes), candidate 2/3 speak time, no hypotheticals, physical/psychological comfort, post-interview dignity to foster positive perceptions even if rejected.
Interrupt for deeper probes.
Show interviewer expertise dominantly.
Keep candidates waiting to test their patience.
questions.explanation: A high-quality selection process that prioritizes candidate dignity and comfort signals a positive organizational culture to the job market.

questions.text: Analyse performance management's angst-inducing tensions for line managers stemming from aligning organisational strategies with individual objectives. Why is it a "strategy implementation tracker" bridging macro-to-micro levels?
options: It merely tracks turnover without goal evolution.

It cascades strategies (e.g., market entry/product launches/channel choices/greenfield/acquisitions) through plans to departmental/team/individual goal sheets (KRAs/KPIs), enables periodic reviews/adjustments for tracking, facilitates feedback, ensures symbiotic organizational/individual growth (continuously upward), and cultivates performance culture via setting/review/appraisal cycles.
It prioritises individual goals over organisational ones.
It eliminates the need for annual appraisals.
questions.explanation: Performance management translates high-level organizational strategies into specific individual goals, allowing the company to track strategy implementation.

questions.text: In a multinational's mid-career marketing team, high performers excel technically but stagnate in one function (high performance/moderate potential quadrant), while high-potential moderate performers seek growth amid war for talent; managers undervalue skills evolution and fail to harness aspirations, leading to exists disrupting context. Evaluate interventions that blend organisational policies with manager empathy to support effective career management.
options: Assess via robust matrix/inputs to differentiate, provide experiences aligning strengths/aspirations/drives with positions, understand level-specific skills, fostering symbiosis where manager/team growth retains assets, substitutes counsellor role via personalised development.
Treat all as vertical ladder climbers only.
Overlook exit costs beyond salary.
Confuse performance with potential universally.
questions.explanation: Effective career management requires managers to differentiate between performance and potential, providing tailored development that aligns individual aspirations with organizational roles.

questions.text: Dissect career as "a series of life experiences" suiting modern shifts (e.g. CA to musicians), emphasising manager engagement with aspirations/experiences amid talent wars/exit costs. Why integrate the performance-potential matrix with the skills framework for development?
options: Enables distinguishing quadrants and potential traits for individualised planning per policies.
Views career solely as an occupation of duration.
Neglects the manager as a career counsellor.
Prioritise pay over growth.
questions.explanation: Integrating performance and potential data allows managers to categorize talent and create individualized development plans that meet modern career expectations.

questions.text: A sales unit faces a severe code breach (harassment) between two reps. Still, involved manager self adjudicates without hearing defense or documenting, issuing abrupt punishments that vary by tenure, resulting in perceived bias, legal challenges, and a culture fear over moral courage. Synthesise the line manager's role in discipline as the fourth HRM-culture interface (selection/performance/development/discipline).
options: Ignore minor norms like timeliness.
Escalate immediately without local resolution.
Self-judge despite involvement.
Uphold natural justice/progressive discipline,transitioning to institutional leadership, harnessing policies for credible culture-building via fiarness in calling out unacceptable acts.
questions.explanation: The manager's role in discipline is to uphold natural justice and progressive discipline, ensuring fairness and organizational credibility.

questions.text: Why does cascading (organisational→ departmental → team → individual goals) with three aspects build culture?
options: Assumes automatic achievement without links.
Applies only to large firms.

Translates strategies/intentions (explicit/implicit) downward for alignment (individual/team/dept/org), providing progress visibility via coaching/review/feedback for self assessment, ensuring goal realisation at base levels achieves apex, cultivating a performance culture universally.
Ends at the team level.
questions.explanation: Cascading goals ensures everyone's objectives are aligned with the company strategy, fostering a shared performance culture.

questions.text: Analyse performance management as a "strategy implementation tracker" through goal cascading (organisation → department → team → individual). Why is it it essential to integrate the three aspects for alignment and progress?
options: Applies solely to articulated large-org strategies.

Ensures that strategies for growth, whether explicit or implicit, are implemented through aligned goals across all levels. Achievements at the individual, team, and department levels contribute to organisational success, providing managers and individuals with clarity and visibility on progress through goal setting, coaching, and feedback reviews. This approach links performance and fosters a strong organisational culture.
Ends at departmental translation.
Omits self-assessment reflection.
questions.explanation: Integration across goal setting, coaching, and review ensures visibility and alignment, linking individual performance directly to organizational success.

questions.text: Analyse goal-setting challenges: intangible factors, interdependencies (90-10% team variance), and up to four results with multiple paths. Why are upfront evaluations, documentation, and periodic reviews critical?
options: . To enhance effectiveness and fairness, an initial metrics discussion facilitates end-of-review evaluations. Documentation tracks modified goals, and quarterly reviews update priorities. Customising approaches based on individual capabilities and interdependencies supports development and rewards, balancing both art and science.
Uniform for all employees.
Ignore team dependencies.
Overload sheets for comprehensiveness.
questions.explanation: Given interdependencies and changing context, documentation and periodic reviews ensure that performance evaluation remains fair and development-oriented.

questions.text: A multinational's sales team includes a novice struggling with basic customer loyalty goals (high interdependency), a performer rubbing stakeholders due to aggressive style, and an expert self-sufficient but needing aspiration exploration; the manager uses uniform directing/teaching, skipping stock taking/reprioritisation/enabling discussions, yielding uneven performance transformations. Evaluate adaptive coaching in performance management.
options: Ignore expertise differences.
Teach uniformly across all.
Skip balanced feedback quarterly.

Tailor styles for different levels: direct for novices, quick feedback for performers, and exploration for experts. Use stock-taking, action changes, and mindful listening to review progress. Ask, "What went right/wrong/better?" Provide balanced feedback quarterly to foster growth and unlock potential, per Whitmore.
questions.explanation: Coaching should be adaptive, offering more direction to novices and exploration to experts to effectively maximize individual performance.

questions.text: A manager rushes through annual reviews due to personal deadlines, texts during sessions without providing self-assessments or behaviour-specific feedback, and argues justifications that lack links to development, creating employee angst and perceived unfairness despite prior coaching. What is the optimal process?
options: Begin self-assessment; specific behaviour feedback linked to development.
Multitask with calls/texts.
Skip preparation/documentation.
Manage 3Cs
questions.explanation: The optimal review process starts with employee self-assessment and focuses on behavior-specific feedback that supports personal development.

questions.text: Team perceives bias in reviews from undocumented episodes, lacking psychological comfort (e.g., standing during calls), with dominant arguing that lacks closure despite goal and coaching preparation. This raises questions about the obligation to be fair.
options: Favour as "nice", not duty

Simplify quarterly coaching by preparing in advance to avoid surprises. Focus on identifying behaviours and discussing expectations. Provide feedback on feelings and actions, explaining their impact on others. Use the 3Cs to ensure fairness and moral responsibility.
Personal bad days first.
Annual surprises post-coaching.
questions.explanation: Fairness is a moral obligation; using regular coaching and identifying specific behaviors prevents bias and ensures psychological comfort.

questions.text: A customer support representative excels in service with outstanding ratings and a long tenure, but lacks knowledge of the product range. The manager sets the same revenue goals as their peers without incorporating cross-sell or upsell strategies, thereby ignoring the representative's aspirations, leading to stagnation. What is the optimal way to integrate reviews and development?
options: Link performance to aspirations: recognise strengths, set ambitious goals, enhance the job and trade present benefits for future versatile growth.
Repeat prior goals.
Ignore multi-year dreams.
Focus incentives only.
questions.explanation: Integrating reviews with development involves linking current performance to individual aspirations to promote long-term engagement and growth.

questions.text: A scaling tech firm has adopted a remote-hybrid work model in response to changing workplace dynamics. However, HR practices remain rigid and office-centric, misaligning with the business strategy for global talent acquisition and hindering stakeholder value, particularly in employee retention. How can optimal insights be applied?
options: Ignore workplace shifts.
Align HR strategy to business strategy, ensuring practices add value and reflect the changing workplace impacts on stakeholder value conceptualisation.
Separate HR from business.
Rigid practices always.
questions.explanation: HR practices must align with business strategy and evolve to reflect the changing nature of the workplace to ensure stakeholder value and talent retention.

questions.text: An Indian entrepreneur in Bhubaneswar sources cobalt for smartphone batteries from Congo, copper wiring from Chile, and assembles in Vietnam for sale to a US brand with no direct factories in those locations. The module emphasises reflecting on everyday products like toothbrushes (140-country company) and coffee (multi-stage global journey) to redefine "global" beyond travel. If export bans on Congolese cobalt are imposed due to ethical mining concerns, how does this MOST challenge the thesis that "each one of us is in some ways a global consumer" for future workers supplying multinationals?
options: Proves consumers can switch to local alternatives without disruption.
Reveals hidden vulnerabilities in global value chains, heightening the need for cross-cultural supplier skills.
Confirms only raw materials are globalised, sparing assembly/marketing.
Undermines Indian multinationals' Fortune 500 status via IT strengths.
questions.explanation: Ethical or geopolitical disruptions highlight the fragility of global supply chains and the vital importance of managing cross-cultural dimension.

questions.text: A manager from Odisha joins Tata (an Indian Fortune 500 multinational) to deliver services to clients in Chile and China, facing north-south Indian cultural gaps internally first. The module urges tracing phone minerals (lithium Argentina/Australia, rare earths China) and urges subculture appreciation (e.g., east-west India differences). What insight BEST equips this manager for engaging "global customers with global employees"?
options: Focusing solely on foreign multinationals ignores Indian giants.
Cultural dimensions scale from India's internal subcultures to international raw material partners.
Everyday reflections, like coffee processing, suffice without formal competencies.
Silicon sourcing trumps rare earths for screen-related negotiations.
questions.explanation: Appreciating internal subcultures (like North-South India) builds the mindset necessary to navigate international cultural differences effectively.

questions.text: An entrepreneur is starting a boutique consulting firm in India. While they do not have offices abroad, their entire "value chain" involves sourcing data from Southeast Asia and selling analytical reports to clients in North America. Based on the assertion that one is global "either as a part of the value chain... or as an employee," which of the following best describes this entrepreneur’s organisational reality?
options: They are a local professional because their physical office is located only in India.
They are an "effective global professional" only if they choose to move their headquarters to a country like the UK or the USA.
They are inherently a global professional because their organisational success depends on navigating cross-cultural dimensions across their value chain.
Their business is exempt from cultural considerations because data analysis is a technical field unaffected by national culture.
questions.explanation: Global status is defined by value chain dependencies, meaning even Indian-based firms are global if they source or sell across borders.

questions.text: The entrepreneur studies the history of the East India Company and the early entry of HSBC and City Bank into India. What is the most relevant "People and Organisation" lesson they can draw from these historical examples for their own venture?
options: High-performing global organisations have historically demonstrated long-term commitment to navigating diverse geographical and cultural landscapes.
Global business is a recent fad that might not last another decade.
Banks are more global than manufacturing companies because they arrived in India earlier.
To be successful, they must wait until after "independence" to start manufacturing their products.
questions.explanation: Being global is an old concept; historical success stories show that longevity requires managing cultural differences over long periods.

questions.text: Warren Buffett's genie lottery with "six billion different Lotto balls" underscores world diversity before defining culture as "norms, ways of behaviour, attitudes, beliefs and values." Why is this tricky setup MOST essential for global professionals, according to the module?
options: To prioritise lottery wins over cultural programming.
To force reflection on unknown starting points, mirroring nationality differences.
To limit culture to economic systems only.
To equate health/intelligence with Hofstede's six dimensions.
questions.explanation: The lottery analogy encourages professionals to recognize that their starting point (nationality/culture) is accidental and shapes their worldview.

questions.text: Hofstede's "shared programming of the mind" is partly unique/partly shared, passed via norms. Which is the trickiest implication for cross-cultural intelligence?
options: It ignores personal reflection on behaviours.
It mandates judging others against your norms.
It centralises effectiveness in personal/professional lives via self-awareness.
It applies only to tribes, not business organisations.
questions.explanation: Cross-cultural intelligence requires deep self-awareness to recognize how one's own 'mental programming' affects interactions with others.

questions.text: Observable differences (speech, dress, music, food, communication) lead to frameworks like Hofstede's first four: power distance, individualism/collectivism, masculinity/femininity, uncertainty avoidance. What's tricky about starting with "differences" before similarities?
options: Differences preclude frameworks.
Food habits trump beliefs/values.
Appreciation of differences enables similarity-seeking in nationalities.
India-US travel skips regional variances.
questions.explanation: By first understanding and respecting deep cultural differences, global professionals can more effectively find common ground and similarities.

questions.text: Power distance describes the extent to which society tolerates and accepts unequal power dynamics between individuals holding authority and those who do not. In India, this concept manifests in a pronounced hierarchical structure, where societal norms are deeply influenced by factors such as age, seniority, and economic status. Conversely, the United States embraces a more egalitarian approach, characterised by a relatively flat hierarchy. It’s essential to understand that there isn’t a definitive "good or bad" attached to these varying cultural perspectives; however, what could be the implications of these differences?
options: US hierarchies erase all inequality.
High power distance aids knowledge-sharing from the experienced to the young.
India defers only to age, not experience.
Conflicts arise solely from low scores.
questions.explanation: High power distance cultures, like India, see the role of seniority as providing wisdom and advice to younger generations.

questions.text: Individualism vs. collectivism: India falls in the middle due to its competitive education system and the personal nature of religion. What makes this a tricky manifestation?
options: Individualism means full personal responsibility; collectivism shares via family/group.
High collectivism always trumps family loyalty.
Low individualism ignores social networks.
Hinduism fosters pure collectivism.
questions.explanation: India displays a mix of traits where individuals take personal responsibility (individualism) while being deeply connected to social networks (collectivism).

questions.text: A student relies entirely on generative AI to draft assignments without prior analysis. Over time, the student’s performance declines. What is the most plausible explanation?
options: AI models are unreliable
Overuse of AI reduces motivation
Lack of domain understanding weakens output quality.
AI discourages originality
questions.explanation: Without tacit domain knowledge, individuals cannot provide the necessary judgment and problem identification that makes technology effective.

questions.text: An organisation automates several operational roles and simultaneously hires data scientists. Which structural risk is most likely if no reskilling occurs?
options: Reduced innovation
Decline in organisational culture
Increased operational costs
Digital divide within the workforce
questions.explanation: Technological advancement without corresponding workforce reskilling leads to a socioeconomic 'digital divide' between workers.

questions.text: A professional checks their phone frequently during complex tasks, leading to slower completion times. This is best explained by:
options: Information overload
Reduced task relevance
Attention fragmentation and recovery cost
Cognitive fatigue from multitasking
questions.explanation: Frequent distractions lead to attention fragmentation; the 'recovery cost' of refocusing on complex tasks significantly slows down work.

questions.text: A company encourages constant digital availability to boost collaboration. Unexpectedly, decision quality declines. Why?
options: Loss of deep focus and reflection
Increased workload
Reduced accountability
Overdependence on communication tools
questions.explanation: Constant digital interruptions rewiring the brain to focus on short-term fleeting memories prevent the deep reflection needed for high-quality decisions.

questions.text: A leader wants employees to use AI ethically and effectively. Which policy would best support this goal?
options: Mandating AI usage in all tasks
Restricting AI access entirely
Encouraging AI use after independent problem framing
Evaluating employees solely on AI-enhanced outputs
questions.explanation: Effective AI use requires humans to first frame problems and apply judgment before leveraging technology for efficiency.

questions.text: Which statement best captures the relationship between ethics and decision-making in organisations?
options: Ethical behaviour is relevant only when decisions violate the law
Ethics applies mainly to individual conduct, not organisational choices
Ethical dilemmas arise because most managerial decisions involve trade-offs
Ethics becomes relevant only when outcomes negatively affect stakeholders
questions.explanation: Most ethical challenges for managers come from the difficult trade-offs inherent in complex organizational decisions.

questions.text: Which of the following distinctions is most consistent with the explanation of ethics provided?
options: Ethics is synonymous with personal values and emotions
Ethics and legality operate at the same threshold
Ethics varies entirely based on cultural norms
Ethics prescribes both what must be avoided and what ought to be done
questions.explanation: Ethical standards serve to both proscribe (avoid harm/wrong) and prescribe (do good/virtuous acts).

questions.text: A postgraduate student submits an assignment that includes AI-generated content without acknowledgement. When questioned, the student argues that the ideas are “public knowledge” and that no explicit rule was violated. Which ethical insight best applies to this situation?
options: Ethical violations occur only when intent to deceive is proven
Ethical judgment depends on peer behaviour in similar situations
Ethical responsibility ends if outcomes are academically acceptable
Personal justification does not override established ethical standards
questions.explanation: Ethical behavior is based on objective standards; personal rationalizations (like 'no explicit rule') do not make an action ethical.

questions.text: A product manager is under pressure to release a software product known to have unresolved safety issues. The justification given is that “every competitor does this” and “the risks are minimal.” Which belief most accurately explains why a good professional might make an unethical choice here?
options: The action is illegal but unavoidable
The activity is perceived as within acceptable ethical limits
The organisation has formal compliance mechanisms
The manager lacks awareness of consequences
questions.explanation: Good managers often make unethical choices because they rationalize the activity as being within 'reasonable' limits or justified by common practice.

questions.text: Why do organisations invest in codes of conduct, whistleblower policies, and ethics committees?
options: To ensure employee obedience
To comply with regulatory minimums
To protect reputation, manage risk, and sustain trust
To standardise employee morale beliefs
questions.explanation: Ethical infrastructure protects a firm's reputation and long-term sustainability by reducing risks of litigation and loss of trust.

questions.text: A manager working across countries encounters differing norms around gift-giving and punctuality. Which approach best reflects ethical maturity in such contexts?
options: Always adopt local practices without question
Apply home-country norms consistently
Balance cultural sensitivity with universal values like dignity and fairness
Avoid making value-based judgments altogether
questions.explanation: Ethical maturity involves navigating local norms while adhering to universal principles like respect and dignity.

questions.text: Self-awareness, as a life skill, underpins effective management and is linked to reflection on past experiences. In the context of increasing environmental complexity and global heterogeneity, analyse which competency synthesis most directly challenges managers to transcend traditional learning/unlearning?
options: Continuously acquiring skills while preserving core competencies that ensured past success.
Deliberately shedding self-believed competencies (e.g., "I should not do this anymore") to create space for innovation beyond confining boundaries.
Building networks reactively only after failures, without reflection.
Examining decisions solely from internal organisational feedback.
questions.explanation: The concept of 'shedding' involves deliberately giving up old competencies to make room for new learning and innovation.

questions.text: Arjun, a functional manager of managers at a manufacturing firm, has built deep expertise over 8 years, personally handling all critical negotiations. His team reports stagnation because he hasn't identified anyone who is even 70% ready to assume his role. A new leadership opportunity arises, but Arjun hesitates, fearing no one can replicate his "irreplaceable" style, which leads to a career plateau despite annual appraisals praising his depth. Evaluate Arjun's situation: What primary personal development agenda item must he prioritise to enable career growth, per the dimensions of personal effectiveness?
options: Invest in team automation for negotiations to free his time, without building successors.
Reflect annually on last year's new learnings and set goals for next year's unknowns, ignoring successor readiness.
Create a successor (at least 70% ready within 1-2 years) by delegating, recognising indispensability signals a plateaued career.
Reproduce his first-year experience yearly for "depth," coaching others only post-appraisal.
questions.explanation: Managers must build successors to avoid becoming 'too indispensable,' which limits their own career progression.

questions.text: Sofia manages a sales team of 8, with 6 high performers driving 80% of results, but two vacant/ underfilled positions in key regions persist due to hiring delays, weakening overall chain strength. She is focusing future initiatives on digital sales, but hasn't assessed internal rotations or gaps in required talent next year. Which managerial effectiveness question, if addressed first, would most directly strengthen her team's vulnerable aspects amid upcoming initiatives?
options: Allocate more time to high-performers for engagement, as they drive results despite vacancies.
Identify key talent gaps (e.g., unfilled positions as weakest links) and plan future people needs via rotations for initiatives.
Provide support only to non-performers, ignoring average contributors.
Reflect on opportunities for stars, assuming average performers self-maintain.
questions.explanation: Managerial effectiveness is determined by the team's weakest link; identifying and closing talent gaps is a priority for chain strength.

questions.text: Karan, a department head, excels in blockchain tech upskilling (domain/technical), securing salary via optimisations, but struggles with feedback delivery, causing team disengagement during reviews. A 360-degree feedback reveals breakdowns in coaching and resource provision; he attributes the issues to aptitude gaps, ignoring attitude in enabling others, despite communication conflicts signalling areas for improvement. Which priority focus elevates his managerial effectiveness, leveraging available feedback mechanisms?
options: Use 360-feedback/conflicts for self-awareness on non-technical attitudes (e.g., enabling via coaching/feedback), applying discipline to behavioural skills like reviews and integrity.
Prioritise regulatory reports and AR/VR tech, as behavioural issues resolve via domain mastery.
Delegate all networking to bosses, focusing solely on tracking/monitoring tech tools.
Upskill in Industry 5.0 software, assuming attitude follows functional expertise.
questions.explanation: Managerial effectiveness relies on attitudes and enabling others through feedback, rather than just technical domain mastery.
