questions.text: A company conducts an internal analysis and finds a skill gap in data interpretation. How can this insight be leveraged strategically?
options: Hire data specialists
Design training programs
Develop strategic partnerships
Invest in automation tools
questions.explanation: Addressing internal skill gaps through targeted training programs builds the necessary capabilities to execute strategy.

questions.text: When two firms possess similar tangible assets, how can one still achieve superior performance?
options: By leveraging superior leadership and culture
By investing more in assets
By restructuring financial policies
By hiring more employees
questions.explanation: Intangible resources like leadership and culture often create the difference in performance when physical assets are equal.

questions.text: In evaluating a firm’s strengths, why is it important to assess imitability?
options: Because it indicates resource value
Because it shows how competitors will respond
Because it identifies causal ambiguity
Because it determines market growth
questions.explanation: Assessing imitability helps predict how quickly rivals will be able to replicate a successful strategy and erode the advantage.

questions.text: A manager conducting a VRIO analysis identifies multiple valuable but non-rare resources. What strategic action should follow?
options: Discontinue resource allocation
Invest in imitation
Reallocate to more distinctive areas
Ignore them
questions.explanation: Non-rare resources only provide competitive parity; a firm should reallocate its focus toward areas that offer unique, rare advantages.

questions.text: A company integrates its marketing, Research and Development, and customer support teams to respond faster to market shifts. What capability is being strengthened?
options: Operational efficiency
Dynamic capability
Learning capability
Cross-functional capability
questions.explanation: The ability to reconfigure internal teams to respond quickly to market changes is a core aspect of dynamic capabilities.

questions.text: After benchmarking, a firm realises it has higher innovation speed but lower cost efficiency. What should be its next strategic step?
options: Focus on reducing costs to match competitors
Capitalise on innovation and reduce inefficiencies
Ignore innovation and cut Research and Development budgets
Invest in aggressive marketing
questions.explanation: Management should double down on its innovative strengths while actively working to minimize its operational weaknesses.

questions.text: An organisation has built strong brand loyalty but faces disruption from technology-based entrants. What internal action will help sustain advantage?
options: Invest in new technology
Leverage customer engagement and service quality
Focus on short-term profits
Increase production capacity
questions.explanation: To survive technological disruption, a firm must modernize its core capabilities despite existing brand strength.

questions.text: What is the primary reason why aligning structure with strategy is critical in organisation
options: It ensures ecient oper ations irrespective of market changes
It enables better alignment between goals, decision-making, and execution
It allows maximum profit optimisation through rigid hierarchy
It minimises the importance of structure in execution
questions.explanation: An organization's structure must be designed to support and facilitate the execution of its strategic goals.

questions.text: According to the A-S-P model, which component ensures continuous learning and adustment
options: Analysis component that monitors external conditions
Strategy component that designs market choices
Performance component that tests strategy execution
Feedback loop that ensures dynamic realignment
questions.explanation: The feedback loop provides the information necessary to adjust strategies and structures based on performance results.

questions.text: A firm’s alanced Scor ecard shows strong process eciency but weak learning metrics What risk does this pose
options: Reduced adaptability to changing environments
Potential skill gap in leadership training
Increased employee resistance
Decline in eciency metrics
questions.explanation: Poor learning and growth metrics indicate that the firm may struggle to innovate and adapt when market conditions shift.

questions.text: Management notices that performance reviews are inconsistent across departments What aspect of performance management needs strengthening
options: Clear goal-setting and calibration
Automated tracking tools
Manager coaching and feedback capability
Employee incentive alignment
questions.explanation: Calibration processes ensure that performance standards are applied consistently across various departments and teams.

questions.text: What is the main dier ence between strategic and financial controls
options: Strategic controls are ualitative and futur e-oriented financial contr ols are uantitative and past-oriented
Strategic controls focus on past results, while financial controls are forward-looking
Strategic controls are rigid and measurable, unlike financial controls
Financial controls are subective while str ategic are uantitative
questions.explanation: Strategic controls monitor the validity of the strategy and its implementation path, while financial controls measure past economic success.

questions.text: A company notices delays in decision-making due to excessive centralisation Which structur al adaptation would best improve agility
options: Increase in hierarchical levels for better control
Delegating more authority to lower levels
Reinforce centralised reporting lines
Reduce team autonomy
questions.explanation: Decentralization by delegating authority empowers local levels to make decisions faster and improves overall organizational responsiveness.

questions.text: A fast-growing startup faces bottlenecks because all approvals go through the founder Which structur e could resolve this
options: Remain in simple structure but add more assistants
Adopt a functional structure with clear department heads
Transition to functional structure for eciency
Shift to a decentralised structure
questions.explanation: Moving to a functional structure with departmental heads helps distribute responsibility and removes the founder as the sole bottleneck.

questions.text: Which element in performance management translates strategy into measurable actions
options: e y Performance Indicators PIs
Performance appraisal system
Organisational hierarchy
Strategic communication channels
questions.explanation: Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) act as the link between broad strategic goals and concrete, trackable operational metrics.

questions.text: A firm implements new digital tools for tracking performance but sees no improvement What could be the reason
options: Misalignment between digital tools and cultural adoption
Lack of manager capability to use data eectively
Overreliance on technology instead of training
Overdependence on consultants
questions.explanation: Technology alone cannot improve performance if the organization's culture and people are not aligned to utilize it.

questions.text: A diversified company’s divisions are competing for funds and customers Which contr ol mechanism would help align them
options: Corporate-level resource allocation and governance
alanced Scor ecard integration
Autonomous divisional planning systems
Greater centralisation of authority
questions.explanation: At the corporate level, management must govern and allocate resources across units to ensure collective organizational goals are prioritized over unit competition.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of applying hypothesis testing in entrepreneurship?
options: To validate business ideas scientifically before investing resources
To replace market research altogether
To guarantee the success of a startup
To eliminate risks entirely
questions.explanation: Hypothesis testing allows entrepreneurs to scientifically verify their assumptions about a business model before committing significant capital or time.

questions.text: Why is understanding scientific methods important for entrepreneurs?
options: It guarantees they will become Nobel laureates
It helps reduce heartache and resource wastage during venture development
It allows entrepreneurs to avoid all risks
It guarantees quick success in the market
questions.explanation: Scientific methods provide a structured way to test ideas, helping entrepreneurs avoid pursuing unviable paths and wasting resources.

questions.text: What is a common misconception about being born an entrepreneur?
options: It’s necessary to be born with a high risk-taking ability
Anyone can learn and develop entrepreneurial skills through principles and methods
Entrepreneurs are only born in wealthy families
Entrepreneurship requires formal education only
questions.explanation: The core teaching is that entrepreneurship is a discipline that can be learned, debunking the myth that it is an innate trait one must be born with.

questions.text: According to Steve Blank, how is a startup best defined?
options: A permanent business with a fixed model
A company designed to fail quickly
A temporary organisation in search of a scalable, sustainable business model
An organisation solely focused on technology development
questions.explanation: Steve Blank defines a startup as a temporary organization designed to search for a repeatable and scalable business model.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the nine building blocks in the business model canvas?
options: Revenue streams
Cost structure
Marketing slogans
Customer segments
questions.explanation: Marketing slogans are promotional tools, not one of the foundational structural blocks like segments, costs, or revenue in the Business Model Canvas.

questions.text: What illustrates a segmented market?
options: Offering vehicles to different customer needs (budget, luxury, utility)
Only selling one universal car model
Having no variations in product design
Ignoring customer preferences
questions.explanation: Market segmentation involves dividing a broad market into subsets of consumers who have common needs or priorities.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a utility lever in the buyer utility map?
options: Environmental friendliness
Risk reduction
Convenience
Financial auditing
questions.explanation: Financial auditing is a corporate compliance process, not a standard lever used to increase buyer utility in a product's value proposition.

questions.text: What are the six experience stages considered in the buyer utility map?
options: Purchase, delivery, usage, supplements, maintenance, disposal
Marketing, sales, distribution, execution, service, follow-up
Planning, development, launch, growth, maturity, decline
Awareness, evaluation, purchase, consumption, retention, advocacy
questions.explanation: The buyer utility map tracks the customer experience through six specific stages: purchase, delivery, usage, supplements, maintenance, and disposal.

questions.text: What does a buyer utility map evaluate?
options: The financial health of a company
How products or services deliver value across utility levers and experience stages
The marketing budget efficiency
The operational workflow
questions.explanation: A buyer utility map helps identify where a product creates value for customers across different levers and stages of the user experience.

questions.text: What triad does the course suggest for framing hypotheses in startups?
options: Problem, product, price (PPP)
Customer, problem, solution (CPS)
Marketing, sales, finance (MSF)
Investor, team, business (ITB)
questions.explanation: The CPS triad ensures entrepreneurs validate who the customer is, what problem they face, and if the proposed solution actually works for them.

questions.text: What does the “blue ocean” strategy primarily focus on?
options: Competing directly in crowded markets
Creating new market space and making competition irrelevant
Lowering costs to beat competitors
Copying competitors' products
questions.explanation: Blue ocean strategy is about creating uncontested market space where competition is non-existent or irrelevant.

questions.text: Which element is the cornerstone of blue ocean strategy?
options: Cost minimisation
Marketing budgets
Competitive spying
Value innovation, creating better value for buyers
questions.explanation: Value innovation, the simultaneous pursuit of differentiation and low cost, is the fundamental principle of blue ocean strategy.

questions.text: What key framework helps companies implement a blue ocean strategy by identifying what to eliminate, raise, reduce, and create?
options: SWOT analysis
ERRC model
Porter’s five forces
PESTLE analysis
questions.explanation: The Eliminate-Reduce-Raise-Create (ERRC) grid is used to reconstruct buyer value elements and create a new value curve.

questions.text: How can entrepreneurs discover potential new categories?
options: By ignoring local market needs
By competing only on price
By observing unmet or underserved customer needs and innovating solutions
By copying successful corporations
questions.explanation: New market categories are found by identifying gaps where current market offerings do not satisfy specific customer needs.

questions.text: Why do smaller but higher-paying customer segments matter to startups?
options: They generate disproportionately higher revenue and loyalty
They are easy to ignore
They require no marketing efforts
They prefer mass-market products
questions.explanation: Niche segments with higher willingness to pay often provide the necessary margins and early adoption loyalty needed for a startup to survive.

questions.text: What was a key reason the venture "Better Place" failed despite its innovative battery swapping network for EVs?
options: Lack of funding
Poor customer service
Too much competition
Bad timing
questions.explanation: Despite the innovation, 'Better Place' failed largely due to the market and infrastructure not being ready at that specific time.

questions.text: Why is it essential for entrepreneurs to "fail fast" or "fail smart"?
options: To minimise losses and gain learning quickly for better future ventures
To avoid taking any risks
To gain media attention
To avoid customer feedback
questions.explanation: Failing fast allows entrepreneurs to test assumptions quickly and pivot or stop before they exhaust their resources.

questions.text: When defining metrics for an experiment, what question should be answered?
options: How many competitors exist?
What is the product price?
How will you measure success?
Who will be the CEO?
questions.explanation: Clear metrics are necessary to determine whether an experimental test has validated or invalidated a specific hypothesis.

questions.text: What is the first step suggested before ideation in starting a venture?
options: Identify a significant problem to solve
Focus on building technology
Pick a famous product
Prepare a business plan
questions.explanation: Successful entrepreneurship starts with finding a real, painful problem rather than just having a product idea without a clear use case.

questions.text: What does "loss aversion" imply when developing products?
options: Customers dislike changing unless the new option is significantly better
People take risks in order to avoid losses
Customers will always choose new products
Customers buy products that are developped using low cost
questions.explanation: Loss aversion means people feel the pain of losing what they have more than the gain of something new, so a new product must be significantly superior to trigger a switch.

questions.text: Why is time spent on site or heat maps useful for startups?
options: They show where customers spend attention, helping refine user experience.
They increase advertising costs
They replace customer interviews
They are not actionable
questions.explanation: Heat maps and time metrics provide behavioral data on where users focus, allowing for data-driven UX improvements.

questions.text: Which metric indicates strong evidence of stakeholder interest in a new venture?
options: Bounce rate
Signup rates and collected contact information
Total website visits only
Number of product features
questions.explanation: Collecting contact information or having signups represents a much higher level of commitment and interest than simple page visits.

questions.text: You receive positive verbal feedback but no follow-up action or contact details from potential customers. What might this indicate?
options: Genuine customer interest
Strong sales potential
Immediate product market fit
False positive due to politeness bias, not actual commitment
questions.explanation: Verbal compliments without commitment are often 'false positives' where people are being polite rather than expressing real purchase intent.

questions.text: What should a startup do upon realising the original customer segment is not interested, but a new segment shows interest?
options: Ignore the new segment
Pivot customer focus and adapt offerings accordingly
Delay pivoting
Expand product features
questions.explanation: Pivoting allows a startup to shift its focus toward a market segment that demonstrates actual demand.

questions.text: Why should startups believe customers over friends, family, or mentors?
options: Customers define market reality
Friends always give unbiased feedback
Mentors are always correct
Family has better insights
questions.explanation: Actual customers are the only ones who provide evidence of whether a product will be purchased, whereas friends and family are often biased.

questions.text: During early testing, why is it important not to push customers for positive signals?
options: Because only positive feedback matters
To speed up experiments
Because negative feedback is irrelevant
To avoid leading questions that bias the results
questions.explanation: Seeking only validation prevents entrepreneurs from seeing flaws and leads to biased data that doesn't reflect true market needs.

questions.text: What should entrepreneurs do if customers partially deny the usefulness of a feature during interviews?
options: Dismiss the feedback
Consider pivoting or refining the feature to match customer needs better
Double down on the feature unthinkingly
Ignore customer segments
questions.explanation: Constructive criticism or partial denial is a signal to refine the product to better align with what the customer actually values.

questions.text: What action shows more commitment than verbal compliments in interviews?
options: Providing an email or phone number for follow-ups
Saying “nice idea” several times
Staying silent
Changing the topic
questions.explanation: Volunteering personal contact information is a 'skin in the game' metric that indicates real interest in the solution.

questions.text: What is the recommended approach when some hypotheses are partially supported?
options: Disregard the data
Refine and iterate to clarify assumptions and retest
Abandon the project immediately
Skip further research
questions.explanation: Entrepreneurship is an iterative process; partial support suggests the idea is close but needs refinement to be fully validated.

questions.text: Why is sunk cost fallacy dangerous in entrepreneurial ventures?
options: It encourages immediate pivoting
It makes entrepreneurs focus on past investments, not present evidence
It ensures success
It promotes innovation
questions.explanation: The sunk cost fallacy leads founders to continue wasting resources on failing ideas just because they have already invested heavily in them.

questions.text: What mindset is crucial for entrepreneurs during hypothesis testing?
options: Proving their assumptions right
Trying to prove themselves wrong to uncover real insights
Ignoring customer feedback
Seeking only positive validation
questions.explanation: The most effective entrepreneurs try to invalidate their ideas quickly to avoid spending time and money on unworkable concepts.

questions.text: During early-stage startups, what is a common source of wasted effort?
options: Creating complex solutions without validating customer problems
Focusing on launching the product
Excessive funding
Lack of technical skills
questions.explanation: Building a full-featured product before confirming that customers actually have the problem it solves is a primary cause of startup failure.

questions.text: How should entrepreneurs treat success milestones?
options: As static end goals
As evolving targets to push further growth
Irrelevant details
A sign to stop working
questions.explanation: Milestones should be used as indicators to iterate and set higher objectives for continuous growth and scaling.

questions.text: What is the role of communities (entrepreneurship cells, incubators) in startup ecosystems?
options: Provide support, learning, and networking opportunities
Increase competition
Distract founders
Only facilitate funding
questions.explanation: Incubators and communities provide the mentorship and resources necessary to nurture a startup through its early vulnerable stages.

questions.text: According to Pradyun, why should entrepreneurs aim to “self-select in” rather than “self-select out”?
options: To avoid challenges
To minimise workload
To wait for perfect conditions
To actively engage and seize opportunities despite difficulties
questions.explanation: Entrepreneurship requires a proactive mindset where you choose to participate and solve problems rather than waiting for conditions to be easy.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true about 'effectuation' and 'causal thinking'?
options: Effectuation involves iterative creation
Effectuation ignores planning
Causal thinking means guessing
Effectuation means random trial and error
questions.explanation: Effectuation is a logic of entrepreneurial expertise that involves working with available means to iteratively create an outcome.

questions.text: A founder feels deeply “passionate” about a specific idea and refuses to consider alternatives even when evidence shows limited urgency and a tiny market size. What is the key risk associated with this form of passion?
options: Passion always guarantees success, so there is no risk
Investors will automatically invest more
Attachment to one idea can block pivoting away from a fundamentally weak problem choice
It eliminates the need for any validation
questions.explanation: Over-attachment to a solution prevents a founder from acknowledging negative data and shifting to a more viable problem/market.

questions.text: Pradyun's group builds a matching algorithm for mentoring rural children with college students during a hackathon. Why was this successful?
options: Team collaboration with diverse skills and clear roles
Only focusing on coding speed
Ignoring mentorship aspects
Over-relying on external vendors
questions.explanation: The combination of technical skills and a clear understanding of the social objective within a collaborative team led to the successful outcome.

questions.text: How did cold emailing help Pradyun in launching his startup efforts?
options: It was a waste of time
It required no effort
Only social media is effective
It helped in networking and finding opportunities proactively
questions.explanation: Proactive outreach like cold emailing allows founders to build a network and find opportunities that aren't publicly advertised.

questions.text: What is more important than the product or idea in entrepreneurship?
options: Execution and delivering value
Marketing campaigns
Funding amounts
Team size
questions.explanation: A great idea is worthless without proper execution and the ability to consistently provide value to the target market.

questions.text: Choose the correct option that best defines climate resilience.
options: The capacity of systems to cope with hazards while maintaining their core functions and the ability to adapt and transform
The process of preventing all climate-related disasters through strict environmental laws
The rapid recovery of economic systems after any natural event
The elimination of social and environmental vulnerability through technology
questions.explanation: Climate resilience refers to the ability of a system to absorb shocks and adapt while maintaining its essential structure and functions.

questions.text: Determine whether the following statement is True or False. Select one of the options listed to select your choice. Small enterprises are characterised by investments up to ₹125 crore and a turnover not exceeding ₹500 crore.
options: True
False
N/A
N/A
questions.explanation: The current classification for small enterprises in India is an investment up to ₹10 crore and turnover up to ₹50 crore.

questions.text: In a drought-prone village, local elders advise farmers to sow seeds only after observing the blooming of a particular wild plant which is a signal that underground moisture is sufficient for germination. A scientific team later confirms that this plant blooms only when soil humidity reaches a threshold ideal for seed growth. The government then decides to include this traditional indicator in official drought forecasting programs. Question: Which statement best explains the significance of this practice?
options: Indigenous knowledge reflects experiential understanding that can complement modern science when validated through observation and evidence.
Indigenous knowledge provides symbolic cultural traditions that have little scientific basis.
Indigenous knowledge is reliable only when replaced by modern scientific forecasting systems.
Indigenous knowledge functions independently of environmental observations and has no practical value.
questions.explanation: Traditional and indigenous knowledge provides valuable insights based on long-term observation that can enhance and complement modern scientific methods.

questions.text: Which of the following statements best distinguishes climate mitigation from climate adaptation?
options: Climate mitigation focuses on adjusting to climate impacts, while climate adaptation works to stop greenhouse gas emissions.
Climate mitigation reduces the effects of climate change, while climate adaptation reduces the causes by cutting greenhouse gas emissions.
Climate mitigation reduces the causes of climate change, while climate adaptation manages its effects to minimize harm or seize opportunities.
Climate mitigation deals only with emergency response after disasters, while climate adaptation prevents emissions through technology.
questions.explanation: Mitigation focuses on addressing the root causes by reducing emissions, whereas adaptation focuses on managing the consequences of climate change.

questions.text: A popular shoe brand launches a new program where customers can return worn-out shoes. Instead of throwing them away, the company grinds the soles into new rubber, uses the fabric to make new uppers, and even repairs lightly used shoes for resale. This initiative reduces waste and the need for fresh raw materials. Which sustainability concept does this initiative represent?
options: Green Economy
Circular Economy
Linear Economy
Low- carbon Economy
questions.explanation: A circular economy aims to eliminate waste by continually reusing, repairing, and recycling resources within the production loop.

questions.text: In water-intensive sectors such as textiles, leather, and food processing, which strategy best promotes resource efficiency and long-term sustainability?
options: Increasing groundwater extraction to maintain production levels during dry seasons
Integrating rainwater harvesting, wastewater recycling, and periodic water audits into production systems
Relying on municipal water supply and focusing mainly on waste disposal measures
Using more freshwater for cleaning and processing to ensure higher product quality
questions.explanation: Resource efficiency in water-intensive industries is best achieved through recycling, harvesting, and regular monitoring to reduce overall consumption.

questions.text: Read the statement below and fill in the blank to complete the sentence meaningfully: ______________ was introduced by Prime Minister Narendra Modi at COP26 in Glasgow on 1 November 2021 as a mass movement for mindful and deliberate utilization, instead of mindless and destructive consumption to protect and preserve the environment.
options: Lifestyle for Environment
Green India Mission
Clean Energy Initiative
Sustainable Living Plan
questions.explanation: LiFE (Lifestyle for Environment) is an initiative launched by India to encourage sustainable living and mindful resource consumption.

questions.text: An MSME involved in electronic repairs aims to enhance its environmental image while complying with sustainability norms. Which of the following actions best represents responsible e-waste management?
options: Selling discarded electronic components to local recyclers without verifying their authorization status
Partnering with certified e-waste recyclers to ensure safe, traceable, and compliant disposal
Reusing damaged parts in new products to minimize waste, even if they fail to meet quality standards
Donating obsolete electronic parts to informal repair workshops for extended use
questions.explanation: Responsible management involves using certified channels to ensure that electronic waste is handled according to environmental and safety regulations.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the changing business environment for MSMEs?
options: It focuses only on lowering production costs and increasing output.
It is increasingly shaped by sustainability goals, green supply chains, and consumer demand for eco-friendly products.
It ignores environmental standards and emphasizes traditional manufacturing.
It restricts MSMEs from accessing international markets due to sustainability norms.
questions.explanation: The modern business landscape for MSMEs is evolving to prioritize sustainability, green procurement, and environmentally conscious consumer preferences.

questions.text: Determine whether the following statement is True or False. Select one of the options listed to select your choice. In India, Small and Medium Enterprises are formally recognized as MSMEs under the MSME Act of 2006, which has largely remained unchanged since its introduction, with the most recent updates implemented in 2020 to reflect post-pandemic recovery needs.
options: True
False
N/A
N/A
questions.explanation: The MSME Act of 2006 governs the sector, and significant updates to definitions and criteria were indeed made in 2020.

questions.text: Which of the following statements best describes how stakeholder mapping contributes to effective strategy implementation?
options: Stakeholder mapping is mainly used to outline internal communication channels without affecting strategic decisions.
Stakeholder mapping identifies key stakeholders, their interests, and engagement needs, enabling collaboration that strengthens strategic outcomes.
Stakeholder mapping identifies key stakeholders and their interests but has limited impact on the effectiveness of strategy execution.
Stakeholder mapping becomes relevant only after a strategy fails or encounters resistance.
questions.explanation: Stakeholder mapping helps identify those affected by or influencing a strategy, ensuring their engagement and alignment with organizational goals.

questions.text: Determine whether the following statement is True or False. Select one of the options listed to select your choice. Decarbonisation is a short-term initiative focused primarily on temporary reductions in fossil-fuel use, without requiring major changes in energy sources or process redesign.
options: True
False
N/A
N/A
questions.explanation: Decarbonisation is a long-term, systemic transition that requires fundamental changes to energy systems and industrial processes.

questions.text: Which of the following is not one of the Panchamrit commitments?
options: Achieving 500 GW of non-fossil energy capacity by 2030
Meeting 50 % of India’s energy requirements from renewable sources by 2030
Introducing a national carbon tax as part of climate policy by 2030
Reducing the carbon intensity of the economy by 45 % by 2030
questions.explanation: The Panchamrit commitments focus on capacity, intensity, and net-zero goals; a specific national carbon tax was not part of the initial five pillars.

questions.text: A mid-sized consulting firm operates out of a busy corporate office with dozens of workstations, constantly running servers, frequent client travel, and a heavy reliance on printed documents. The firm wants to start reducing its carbon footprint but cannot commit large upfront investments. Which option represents the most viable path for the firm to initiate decarbonisation efforts?
options: Prioritising a complete overhaul of office interiors with eco-labelled furniture and postponing energy-related upgrades to a later phase
Procuring renewable electricity, shifting to energy-efficient equipment, and moving paperwork and workflows to digital platforms to cut immediate energy and material use
Maintaining current energy systems but compensating emissions entirely through staff-led tree-planting drives and voluntary carbon-offset contributions
Reducing business travel while increasing on-site working hours to optimise office energy use without altering existing equipment or procurement patterns
questions.explanation: Improving efficiency through digitalization and renewable energy procurement offers immediate carbon reductions with manageable investment for service firms.

questions.text: __________ refers to achieving a balance between the greenhouse gases released into the atmosphere and those that are removed or absorbed.
options: net zero
carbon neutrality
emission balance
climate parity
questions.explanation: Net zero is the state where the amount of greenhouse gas emitted is equal to the amount removed from the atmosphere.

questions.text: A mid-sized food processing SME has identified multiple sources of emissions. It operates diesel powered delivery vehicles, purchases electricity from a coal-based grid, and sources packaging materials from an external supplier whose production process generates significant emissions. Which category of emissions is correctly matched to the activities of the enterprise?
options: Diesel burned in delivery vehicles is Scope 3, electricity purchased from the grid is Scope 1, and supplier-related emissions are Scope 2
Diesel burned in delivery vehicles is Scope 1, electricity purchased from the grid is Scope 2, and supplier-related emissions are Scope 3
Diesel burned in delivery vehicles is Scope 2, electricity purchased from the grid is Scope 3, and supplier-related emissions are Scope 1
Diesel burned in delivery vehicles is Scope 1, electricity purchased from the grid is Scope 3, and supplier-related emissions are Scope 2
questions.explanation: Scope 1 covers direct emissions, Scope 2 covers indirect emissions from purchased electricity, and Scope 3 includes all other indirect value chain emissions.

questions.text: Determine whether the following statement is True or False. Select one of the options listed to select your choice. GRI Standards are issued by SEBI in India and are mandatory for all companies under the BRSR framework.
options: True
False
N/A
N/A
questions.explanation: GRI Standards are international reporting frameworks; SEBI issues the BRSR framework specifically for Indian listed companies.

questions.text: Choose the correct option that best explains why different sectors require different decarbonisation pathways.
options: Because most sectors generate emissions through broadly similar activities, and applying one standardised strategy reduces complexity and improves consistency

Because sectors such as manufacturing, retail, logistics, construction, and services operate with different emission profiles, resource dependencies, and technological constraints that influence how decarbonisation must be approached
Because service-oriented sectors do not have meaningful carbon footprints, making decarbonization necessary only for energy-intensive industries
Because the success of decarbonization is determined primarily by government regulation, leaving operational differences between sectors largely irrelevant
questions.explanation: Emission reduction strategies must be sector-specific because each industry faces unique operational challenges and technological limitations.

questions.text: Reporting enables SMEs to build ______________ by demonstrating that they are responsibly managing environmental risks and aligning with sustainability expectations.
options: competitive visibility
trust and transparency
regulatory assurance
stakeholder compliance
questions.explanation: Transparency in reporting fosters trust among stakeholders, including investors and customers, regarding a company's environmental impact.

questions.text: Which of the following statements best describes the role of SBTi?
options: It provides companies with voluntary sustainability disclosures that highlight environmental commitments without requiring alignment with climate science
It helps companies set greenhouse gas reduction targets that are consistent with climate science and aligned with the goals of the Paris Agreement
It sets general environmental guidelines for businesses that focus mainly on improving corporate image rather than meeting scientific benchmarks
It focuses exclusively on creating carbon offset projects for large multinational corporations
questions.explanation: The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) ensures that corporate carbon reduction targets are scientifically rigorous and support global temperature limits.

questions.text: Which statement best explains climate adaptation for SMEs?
options: It refers to actions taken only after climate risks disrupt business operations and require urgent response
It involves forward-looking adjustments, including technological, operational, and ecological strategies to prepare for future climate risks
It primarily means purchasing external insurance protection against extreme weather events and natural disasters
It focuses on reducing emissions through carbon credits and offsetting rather than changing business practices
questions.explanation: Adaptation is a proactive approach involving adjustments to operations and strategies to reduce vulnerability to future climate impacts.

questions.text: A small manufacturing enterprise is exploring ways to reduce climate-related risks. However, the owner cannot secure affordable loans, lacks the technical expertise to assess suitable machinery upgrades and is unsure how to access available government support schemes. Which set of interventions would enable the SME overcome barriers to climate adaptation?
options: A mandate requiring SMEs to adopt climate-resilient technologies without providing any financial or technical support
Access to affordable finance, technical guidance on suitable technologies, and government incentives
Access to technical know-how on suitable technologies and information on government schemes
Waiting until climate risks disrupt operations and then applying for emergency relief schemes
questions.explanation: Overcoming adaptation barriers requires a combination of financial support, technical knowledge, and institutional incentives.

questions.text: If an SME prioritises measures such as backup power systems and emergency recovery plans, it can remain climate-secure even without adopting long-term strategies such as resource-efficient technologies or redesigned supply chains. Determine whether the statement is True or False.
options: True
False
N/A
N/A
questions.explanation: Short-term recovery plans do not provide long-term climate security; structural changes in resource use and supply chains are essential.

questions.text:
A millet-processing SME is facing repeated crop losses due to delayed monsoons, heatwaves, and rising

irrigation costs. Retail partners are demanding reliable supply, but farmers in the SME’s network are

struggling to maintain yields and incomes. The SME considers four strategies:

A. Invest in deeper borewells and high-capacity pumps to ensure irrigation even during heatwaves.

B. Shift to heat-resilient millet varieties, adopt sensor-based drip irrigation, and use weather advisory

systems to plan sowing.

C. Maintain current crop practices but secure climate insurance and short-term grain imports to meet supply

commitments.

D. Introduce drought-tolerant crops and provide insurance coverage, without changing irrigation or farming

methods.

Which option best represents the strongest long-term climate adaptation strategy for the SME?
options: Invest in deeper borewells and high-capacity pumps to ensure irrigation even during heatwaves.
Shift to heat-resilient millet varieties, adopt sensor-based drip irrigation, and use weather advisory systems to plan sowing.
Maintain current crop practices but secure climate insurance and short-term grain imports to meet supply commitments.
Introduce drought-tolerant crops and provide insurance coverage, without changing irrigation or farming methods.
questions.explanation: Using resilient crop varieties and water-efficient irrigation technology represents a holistic and sustainable long-term adaptation strategy.

questions.text: Determine whether the following statement is True or False. For SMEs in the EV ecosystem, recycling used batteries is sufficient to ensure both environmental adaptation and economic resilience, as it eliminates the need for second-life battery applications and mineral recovery systems.
options: True
False
N/A
N/A
questions.explanation: A resilient EV ecosystem requires more than just recycling; it must include comprehensive mineral recovery and second-life battery use to be truly sustainable.

questions.text: The ability of an SME to withstand disruptions such as floods, heatwaves, or supply chain failures while continuing operations is referred to as __________.
options: resilience
mitigation
adaptation
sustainability
questions.explanation: Resilience is the specific ability to persist and maintain core functions despite external shocks and disruptions.

questions.text:
A mid-sized packaging enterprise supplies printed cartons to three large FMCG brands. During a severe

heatwave, the industrial zone where all three suppliers operate faced rolling power cuts for over a week. Two

competitors had to stop production entirely. This enterprise however, quickly shifted part of its printing

operations to a rented facility in a neighbouring district and sourced board material from a backup supplier.

As a result, it delivered all orders on schedule. Three weeks later, one FMCG client renewed its annual

contract into a 3-year agreement and shortened the payment cycle from 60 to 30 days.

Which of the following best explains why the FMCG client improved contract terms for the SME?
options: The SME offered limited pricing adjustments during the disruption, making its products temporarily more commercially attractive than competitors
The SME maintained basic compliance and standard operating procedures, demonstrating that it could function without major structural changes during climate stress
The SME showed it could sustain on-time deliveries by rapidly shifting production and sourcing when competitors were forced to halt operations
The SME prioritised regulatory and documentation requirements, which reassured the client that contractual obligations would be formally fulfilled
questions.explanation: Demonstrated operational resilience and reliability during a crisis build high levels of trust and commercial value for buyers.

questions.text: Which of the following is the best example of resilience in an SME?
options: Changing product design to reduce carbon emissions over the next 10 years
Continuing operations during a flood by using backup suppliers and trained staff
Waiting for government relief before restarting production after a heatwave
Focusing only on demand forecasting and ignoring climate disruptions
questions.explanation: Maintaining operations through the use of backup systems and prepared staff during an active hazard is a hallmark of resilience.

questions.text: Why is it important for SMEs to integrate both adaptation and resilience into their climate strategies instead of relying on only one of them?
options: Because adaptation ensures quick recovery after disruptions, while resilience focuses on long-term structural changes
Because adaptation reduces exposure to future risks, while resilience enables businesses to continue operating during shocks, making both necessary for comprehensive protection
Because resilience alone can fully prevent climate-related damage if supported by insurance schemes
Because adaptation and resilience are interchangeable approaches that eventually lead to the same outcomes
questions.explanation: Adaptation and resilience work together to minimize future risks and ensure immediate operational survival during crises.

questions.text: Which of the following best explains how environmental clauses from international buyers are transforming Indian SME operations?
options: They primarily require SMEs to publish CSR reports, but do not affect production or sourcing decisions
They focus only on carbon reporting, allowing SMEs to maintain existing machinery and energy systems
They require SMEs to modify procurement, energy use, production processes, and documentation practices to meet verifiable sustainability expectations
They affect only large firms, while SMEs are exempt from sustainability audits and contract level compliance
questions.explanation: International buyers demand rigorous compliance and verifiable sustainability practices across the entire value chain of their suppliers.

questions.text: Which of the following best reflects the aim of India’s National Wind–Solar Hybrid Policy?
options: To promote large renewable projects by expanding solar parks without integrating wind systems
To develop large grid-connected renewable projects that combine wind and solar power for better efficiency
To create large grid-connected renewable projects by scaling up solar parks alongside wind installations, without requiring hybrid integration
To shift investment away from hybrid systems and focus only on standalone solar development
questions.explanation: The hybrid policy promotes the integration of wind and solar power to optimize land use and improve the efficiency of the power grid.

questions.text: Which interpretation most accurately reflects the pattern of climate reporting in Asia as discussed in Module 4?
options: Climate reporting tends to intensify when governments announce long-term climate frameworks, since such policy documents dominate the environmental news cycle in most Asian outlets

Climate coverage surges predominantly during extreme weather calamities or high-visibility global summits, revealing an episodic and event-driven pattern of reporting rather than sustained climate journalism
Climate stories appear consistently throughout the year because Asian media maintain dedicated climate desks that ensure continuous coverage of slow-onset environmental changes
Climate attention often diminishes during severe environmental crises, as newsrooms reallocate space to political negotiations and sectoral economic updates
questions.explanation: Media coverage of climate in Asia is frequently reactive, spiking during disasters or summits rather than providing consistent, long-term reporting.

questions.text: The UNFCCC refers to Climate Change adaptation as changes in
options: Policy, practices, and structures
Processes, policy, and structures
Processes, practices, and structures
Practices, policies, and structures
questions.explanation: The UNFCCC definition emphasizes adjustments in processes, practices, and structures to address climate change impacts.

questions.text: Determine whether the following statement is True or False. According to the author of the book – Media and Climate Change: Making Sense of Press Narratives - the challenge for developing nations like India is to establish links between development and climate keeping the MDGs as framework to enhance the transition toward a low-carbon economy.
options: True
False
N/A
N/A
questions.explanation: Bridging developmental priorities with climate action is a key focus for media narratives and policy in developing countries like India.

questions.text: Determine whether the following statement is True or False. The UNFCCC is the primary international intergovernmental forum for negotiating the global response to climate change.
options: True
False
N/A
N/A
questions.explanation: The UNFCCC serves as the main global framework for international climate treaties and negotiations.

questions.text: _________________ is the United Nations body for assessing the science related to climate change.
options: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC)
United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP)
World Meteorological Organization (WMO)
Green Climate Fund (GCF)
questions.explanation: The IPCC is the leading international body for the assessment of climate change science.

questions.text: Based on the arguments presented in the book - Media and Climate Change: Making Sense of Press Narratives, which interpretation best explains why vulnerable communities affected by climate change in India remain largely absent from mainstream media narratives?
options: Because the media primarily functions as a neutral historical record, and its limited engagement with disadvantaged groups simply reflects their minimal role in broader public discourse

Because political influences on media organisations encourage reliance on official narratives, while disadvantaged groups despite their environmental proximity remain excluded due to lack of access to digitisation
Because communities closest to the environment tend to hold only traditional knowledge that cannot be integrated into contemporary climate communication frameworks
Because public interpretation of climate news depends mainly on consumer trends, making inclusion of marginalised communities unnecessary for shaping climate narratives
questions.explanation: Media narratives are often constrained by official viewpoints and lack of digital access for marginalized communities, excluding their perspectives from the discourse.

questions.text: Which underlying factor explains why firms with long-standing dependence on plastics and fossil-fuel models hesitate to transition toward sustainability?
options: Reluctance to experiment with new technologies, which creates slow and uneven innovation across the sector
A deeply entrenched focus on short-term financial gains, which often overrides commitments to broader People–Planet–Profit objectives
Regulatory pressure pushing companies to adopt sustainability measures more quickly than market conditions allow
Increasing consumer demand for sustainable alternatives, which reduces incentives for companies to maintain traditional production practices
questions.explanation: The prioritization of short-term profits over long-term sustainability goals often hinders the transition away from fossil-fuel-dependent models.

questions.text: The ‘+’ in REDD+ stands for
options: additional forest-related activities that protect the climate
additional area-related activities that protect the climate
additional climate-related activities that protect the forest
additional climate-related activities that protect the area
questions.explanation: The '+' in REDD+ refers to the role of conservation, sustainable management of forests, and enhancement of forest carbon stocks.

questions.text:
A Bengaluru-based start-up manufactures premium cold-pressed juices sold through e-commerce and cafes.

Until now, the company has used durable plastic bottles because they are cheap, lightweight, and convenient for long-distance delivery. However, three new developments create pressure:

· Delivery apps introduce a “sustainable choice” badge, and products packaged in plastics do not qualify.

· Urban Gen Z customers on social media are increasingly criticising brands that advertise “natural” products while using plastic packaging.

· The Karnataka government announces that it is considering state-level restrictions on certain single-use plastics within the next 18 months.

The founder wants to protect the brand’s image without causing a sudden cost spike that would force juice prices to go up and drive away customers in a competitive market.

Which of the following actions best represents a balanced approach?
options: Replace all plastic bottles immediately with premium glass packaging, signalling strong environmental intent even if logistics become more expensive and operationally difficult
Introduce recyclable or compostable packaging in selected high-volume products first, test cost and customer response, and phase out current plastics in a planned manner
Keep existing plastic packaging until regulations legally mandate a change, then carry out a full switch to whichever alternative seems cheapest at that time
Shift to glass or aluminium packaging for a few niche products only, but continue marketing all products as “eco-friendly” to maintain brand perception without major operational changes
questions.explanation: A phased approach allows a company to manage costs and evaluate market viability while working toward sustainability goals.
